text: AME (SAINT), a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Vosges, arrond. of Remirement. Pop.
AMEDABAD. See AHMEDABAD.
AM-EIS, a village of Illyria, in the circle of Klagenfurt, 12 m. E of Volkermarkt, on the l. bank of the Drave.
AMEGLIA, a village of Sardinia, on the Magra, 3 m. E of Lerici. Pop. 752.
AMEIXIAL, a village of Portugal, in the prov. of Alemtejo, 22 m. NE of Evora.
AMEL, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Meuse, arrond. of Montmedy. Pop. 547.- Also a v. of Prussia, in the gov. of Aix-la-Chapelle, 7 m. NNE of St. Vith.

AMELANDT, an island of Holland, belonging to the prov. of Friesland, separated from the main by a channel about 6 m. in breadth. It is about 12 m. in length from ENE to WSW, and 3 m. in breadth; and has a pop. of about 2,000, chiefly fishers and graziers. There are three villages on the island, namely, Hollum, Ballum, and Nes.
AMELCOOY, a town of La Plata, on the Rio Grande, near the confluence of the San Lorenzo.

AMELIA, a canton and town in the States-of-the- Church, prov. of Spoleto, on a fertile district between the Nera and the Tiber, 18 m. SW by W of Spoleto. Pop. 5,500. The town is the see of a bishop; and is supposed to occupy the site of the ancient Armeria.— Also a county in Virginia, U.S., on the Appomattox. Area 300 sq. m. Pop. in 1840, 10,320. The cap. of the same name is 45 m. SW of Richmond.

AMELIA ISLAND, an island of Florida, in Nassau co. It is separated from the main on the W by a creek at the mouth of St. John's river. It is about 15 m. in length from N to S, by 4 in breadth.—There is another island of the same name off the coast of
AMELIETH, a village of Hanover, near Lauenförda, celebrated for its glass-works.
AMENAR, a town of Spain, in Old Castile, near the source of the Douro, 17 m. SE of Soria.

AMENDOLARA, a small town of Naples, in the prov. of Calabria, near the mouth of a small stream supposed to be the ancient Acalander. "Its position on an insulated cone, surrounded on all sides but one by a precipice of frightful depth and steepness, renders it almost inaccessible." Pop. somewhat under 1,000.
AMENIA, a township in Dutchen co., New York, U.S., 20 m. ENE of Poughkeepsie, to the NE of the Fishkill range. Marble, magnetic ore, and mineral springs occur here. Pop. 2,179.
AMENIAVILLE, a village in the above township, 25 m. ENE of Poughkeepsie. Pop. 200.
AMENY, an islet in the Laccadive group, in N lat. 11° 6½, E long. 72° 57'. It is of a circular form, and does not exceed 3 m. in circuit.
AMER (Point), a cape on the E side of Majorca, in N lat. 39° 34′, E long. 3° 27′.
AMERABAD, a town of Khorasan, 50 m. NW of Meshid. It was fortified by Riza Kuli Khan with a glacis, deep ditch, and wall with bastions, but was taken by Abbas Mirza in 1832.
AMERDINGEN, a town of Bavaria, in the Rezat circle, on the frontiers of Wurtemberg, 12 m. S of Noerdlingen. Pop. 657.

AMERICA. ONE of the great divisions of the globe, extending from nearly the 54th parallel of S to the 72d of N lat.; or from Cape Froward, on the strait of Magalhaens, in S lat. 53° 53′ 43", W long. 71° 14′ 31" [King]—which is 19 degrees further S than Cape Agulhas, the southern extremity of the African continent-to Point Barrow, on the Arctic ocean, NE of Behring's straits, in N lat. 71° 23½, W long. 156° 20'. [Dease and Simpson.] A line drawn between these two capes would exceed 10,000 m. in length; but at either extremity of the continent an extensive archipelago exists by means of which the extreme points of America may be carried two degrees farther S, or to Cape Horn in S lat. 55° 58' 41", W long. 67° 10' 53" [King]; and to the 78th parallel, or perhaps still farther within the Arctic circle at the opposite extremity. The most eastern point of America is Cape Branco, a projecting headland on the Brazilian coast, in S lat. 7° 8' 22", W long. 34° 27'; the most western is Cape Prince of Wales on Behring's straits, in N lat. 65° 33' 30", E long. 167° 59' 10" [Beechey], which is 50 m. distant from East cape the nearest point of Asia. Its greatest breadth is about the 51st northern parallel, where it measures 3,300 m. from the coast of Labrador to the coast of Oregon; and, to the S of the equator, between the coast of Brazil and the coast of Peru, under the 5th parallel, it attains a nearly equal breadth. In the isthmus of Darien or Panama—which may be considered as separating this vast continuous main of land into two distinct continents, the northern and the southern, by a line of demarcation much more defined than that which separates Europe from Asia, or Asia from Africa—the breadth of the American continent is contracted under the 9th northern parallel, and between the Pacific and the Atlantic oceans, to about 20 m.: so that the whole may be regarded as composed of two vast peninsular continents, presenting some remarkable points of resemblance in their general outline as well as physical arrangement; for both have their greatest breadth in the northern part, and both contract, in a pear-like form, towards their southern extremity; in both a vast chain of mountains runs throughout the entire length of each peninsula near the western coast; and in both two vast rivers bear the tribute of a hundred majestic streams, collected from nearly the whole breadth of the continent, to the ocean on the NE and the SE; the St. Lawrence of the northern continent corresponding to the still mightier Amazon of the southern; and the larger Mississippi of the former to the Rio de la Plata of the latter. In both continents, it thus appears, the general declination of the surface is to the SE and to the NE; and each has one great central plain falling in these directions. In their climate and productions, however, and probably in their geological constitution, the regions of North and South America will be found very dissimilar from each other. Area.] Taking the average breadth of the American continent at 2,000 m., and its length at 9,000 m., we might roughly estimate its superficies at 18,000,000 sq. m.; but this is certainly above the actual admeasurement. Balbi supposes it may amount to 11,146,000 sq. m. of 60 to a degree, or 14,790,000 sq. m. of 69.15 to a degree, i.e., English sq. m. Berghaus makes the area 14,219,967 sq. m. without the islands, whose total superficies he estimates at 98,660 sq. m. Gräberg estimates the whole at 15,737,000 sq. m.; Hassel at 17,303,000 sq. m. The fact is, all these admeasurements can only be regarded as so many approximations to the real area, for the actual form and extent of many portions, and the outline of the coast in many places, is yet unknown; and of the extent of territory within the Arctic circle we can only indulge in conjecture. It seems probable that the area of the whole mainland and islands, including Greenland, does not exceed 15,000,000 sq. m., or about one-half of the conjoined superficies of Europe, Asia, and Africa. The greatest length of North America, measured from Cape Lisburn on the Arctic ocean to the SE extremity of Florida, is 3,672 m.; or measured by a line at right angles to that of its greatest breadth, 2,880 m.; and its greatest breadth from Cape Charles in Labrador to the coast of Mexico, near Villa del Fuerte, is 2,800 m. Its superficial area may amount to 7,400,000 sq. m. The greatest length of South America from the coast of Magdalena on the N to Cape Froward on the S is 4,000 m.; or at right angles with the line of its greatest breadth 2,800 m.; and its greatest breadth from Cape St. Roque or Cape Branco, in Brazil, to the point of Malabrigo in Peru is 2,625 m. Its superficial area may amount to 6,500,000 sq. m. Coasts.] The boundaries of this vast continent may be described in general terms as formed by the Atlantic on the one side, separating it from Europe and Africa, and by the Pacific on the other, separating it from Asia and Australia. It approaches nearest to Europe under the 70th northern parallel, where the coast of Greenland is within 900 m. of that of Norway. In the extreme NW it approaches, as we have already said, to within 50 m. of the Asiatic continent, measured across the narrowest part of Behring's straits. Under the parallel of 30° N lat. the coast of Florida is 4,200 m. distant from the W coast of Africa; and to the S of the equator, Rio Janeiro is at an equal distance from the W coast of Africa on the same parallel; while the southern extremity of the African continent is 4,600 m. distant from the mouth of the Rio de la Plata nearly on the same parallel. By still greater distances are the western coasts of America separated from those of China measured across the N Pacific ocean, and the eastern coast of Australia measured across the intervening S Pacific. The average width of the Pacific between the Tropics is 8,500 m.; and the distance of Sandy cape, on the coast of Australia, from the nearest point of S America, on the coast of Chili, is about 8,200 m. The entire length of the coast-line of America is probably little under 34,000 m.; but in the sinuosities of its coast, and the number and magnitude of its bays, gulfs, and straits, the northern portion of this great continent presents a striking contrast to the comparatively compact outline of the southern peninsula. Commencing on the NW, at Cape Prince of Wales on Behring's straits, we have a low swampy shore, and shallow coast, to the entrance of Kotzebue sound, where a few rounded hills appear scattered along the coast, interspersed with small lakes and inlets of the sea. Kotzebue sound runs up to Escholtz bay in 163° W, and a great part of the shore both of the sound and bay is made up of a diluvial formation. From the head of this sound, the coast trends NNW to Cape Lisburne, the abrupt extremity of a rugged range of limestone mountains; whence to Icy cape, it runs NE, presenting a swampy and almost impassable coast, skirted by a chain of sandy islands, and in some places rising in clay cliffs to about 50 ft. in height. From Icy cape to Point Barrow, 126 m. from Icy cape, the coast-line pursues the same direction, and exhibits the same external character. The coast between Cape Barrow and Cape Beechey was first explored by Messrs. Dease and Simpson in 1837. Point Barrow is a long low spit of gravel and coarse sand, which the pressure of the ice has forced up into numerous mounds. From this point to Cape Beechey, a distance of 146 m., the coast runs ESE, presenting a succession of low sand banks and irregular bays, amongst which are Elson's bay, Smith bay, and Harrison bay, without a rock in situ, or boulder stone. From Cape Beechey to the mouth of the Mackenzie river, a distance of above 300 m., the coast still pursues an ESE direction. This portion of the coast was explored by Captain Franklin in 1826-7, and subsequently by Messrs. Dease and Simpson, who descended the western mouth of the Mackenzie river, which Franklin had sought for in vain. It presents a low coast line, with a succession of shallow bays, without a harbour in which a ship could find shelter. Here and there a few clumps of white sprucefir, or some straggling black spruce, were met with at a distance of from 20 to 30 m. from the sea, in sheltered situations; but the only plants which reached the coast were the common juniper, two species of willows, the dwarf-birch, the alder, and a few small shrubby plants. The delta of the Mackenzie river exhibits a wide extent of flat alluvial lands, divided into islands by inosculations of the channels of the river. Beyond it the coast is generally flat and sandy, with here and there sandy hummocks rising abruptly to the height of 100 ft. The mouths of several large rivers have been seen to the eastwards of the Mackenzie river; and the existence of a large lake communicating with the east branch of the Mackenzie, and receiving several large rivers, ascertained. From the Mackenzie river to Cape Bathurst, in N lat. 70° 36', W long. 127° 35', the most northerly part of the main shore between that river and Coppermine river, the general direction of the coast is NE by E. Cape Bathurst is the western extremity of a deep bay, to which the name of Franklin's bay was given, and of which Cape Parry forms the eastern extremity. The western side of this bay presents precipitous banks, similar in structure to the bituminous shale-cliffs at Whitby in Yorkshire; the eastern side, in some places, presents a bold shore, consisting of precipices of limestone rising to 40 or 50 ft., with 3 or 4 fath. water at the base. Cape Parry rises to a height of 700 ft.; and the coast-line to the E of it bears SE by S, exhibiting a succession of curiously perforated limestone cliffs. At Cape Lyon, in N lat. 69° 463', W long. 122° 51', the rise and fall of the tide is short of 20 inches. The coast now becomes low and sandy, with a range of hills running at a distance of from 4 or 5 m. parallel to the coast. Near Cape Clifton, and thence eastwards to Point Wise, the coast consists of horizontal limestone cliffs, separated here and there by shelving beaches. Cape Bexley in 68° 58' N, and 115° 47' W, likewise consists of horizontal beds of limestone. Opposite to this cape is a large island called Wollaston's land; and the intermediate channel, called the Dolphin and Union straits, leading into Coronation gulf, is probably a principal opening though an extensive archipelago in this quarter. From the mouth of the Coppermine river to Cape Barrow, the coast is of moderate elevation, and skirted with rocky barren islands. At Cape Barrow the coast-line again turns to the SE, and runs down as low as <math>66\frac{1}{2}</math>, forming a deep gulf studded with islands, indented with sounds, and presenting many secure bays and harbours, into almost all of which fall rivers of fresh clear water. Though the position of Cape Turnagain is only 61 degrees E of the mouth of the Coppermine river, yet so deeply indented is the coast between these points, that the actual distance sailed by Captain Franklin's adventurous little party, who first explored it, was estimated at 555 geographical miles.—On the 17th of July 1838, Messrs. Dease and Simpson left the mouth of the Coppermine river, and on the 9th of August doubled Cape Flinders; but their advance with boats being stopped by the ice, on the 20th of August, in N lat. 68° 16' and W long. 109° 2', Mr. Simpson pursued his journey on foot, with five servants and two Indians. Each man's load at starting weighed about half a cwt., and their daily progress averaged 20 geographical m. About the middle of the first day's journey they passed the farthest point to which Franklin walked in 1821. Beyond that, the coast preserved its NNE trending to a low cape which was named Cape Franklin. From W to NE a new land or crowded chain of islands of great extent, in many places high and covered with snow, stretched along at the distance apparently of 30 m., and led to the apprehension that they were entering a deep sound or inlet. The mainland now turned off to ENE, which continued to be nearly its general bearing for the three following days. It is flat in its outline, the path leading alternately over soft sand, sharp stones, and swampy ground. At the distance of from 1 to 2 m., the coast is skirted by a range of low stony hills, partially clothed with dull verdure, which send down to the sea numberless brooks and small streams. None of the latter at that season reached above the waist, though the deep and rugged channels of many of them showed that in spring they must be powerful torrents. "The ice all along lay immoveably aground upon the shallow beach, and extended in every direction as far as the eye could reach. The great northern land still stretched out before us," says Mr. Simpson, "and kept alive doubts of our being engaged in exploring an immense bay, which even the increase in the tides, the quantity of sea-weed and shells, and the discovery of the remains of a large whale and of a polar bear, could not altogether dispel. These doubts seemed almost converted into certainty, as we drew near on the fourth evening an elevated cape, and saw land apparently all round. With feelings of bitter disappointment I ascended the height, from whose summit a splendid and most unlooked-for view suddenly burst The ocean, as if transformed by enchantment, rolled its free waves beneath, and beyond the reach of vision to the eastward; islands of various shape and size overspread its surface: and the northern land terminated in a bold and lofty cape bearing NE, at least 40 m. distant; while the coast of the continent trended away to the SE. I stood in fact on a remarkable headland, at the eastern entrance of an ice-obstructed strait. The extensive land to the northward I have called Victoria Land in honour of our youthful sovereign, and the eastern extremity Cape Pelly after the governor of the honourable company. To the promontory where we encamped I have attached the name of Cape Alexander. The rise and fall of the tide here was about 3 ft., being the greatest observed by us in the Arctic seas. The coast here changes its character, the water becomes deep, the approach easy, and I have little doubt that the islands contain secure harbours for shipping. Next morning, at the distance of 8 or 9 m., we crossed another high cape, formed of trap-rocks, in lat. 68° 52' N, the variation of the compass being 63° E. The travelling had become more and more toilsome, our road now passing over some miles of round loose stones; and then through wet mossy tracts sown with large boulders and tangled with dwarf willows. At our usual camping hour we opened a large bay, studded with islands, which ran in for 5 m. to the SSW, and then turned off in a bold sweep of rounded granitic hills dipping to the sight in the ESE at the dis- The morning of the 25th of Aug. was devoted to the determination of our position, and the erection of a pillar of stones on the most elevated part of the point; after which I took possession of the country, with the usual ceremonial, in the name of the hon. company, and for the queen of Great Britain. The I. situation of this pillar is in N lat. 68° 44', W long. 106° 3'; the magnetic variation being 60° E. The compass grew sluggish and uncertain in its movements as we advanced eastward, and frequently had to be shaken before it would traverse at all. Two miles to the southward of our encampment, a rapid river of some magnitude discharges itself into the bay, the shores of which seemed more broken and indented than those along which we had travelled. Independently of Victoria Land, and an archipelago of islands, I have had the satisfaction of tracing fully 100 m. of coast, and of seeing 30 m. farther, making in all about 120 m. of In 1839 Messrs. Dease and Simpson resumed their exploration continental journey." of the northern coast of America. From Cape Alexander in N lat. 68° 56', W. long. 106° 40', to another remarkable point in N lat. 68° 33', W long. 93° 10', the Arctic coast may be considered as forming one spacious bay stretching as far S as 67° 40'. This vast sweep is indented by an endless succession of minor bays, separated from each other by long narrow points of land, and enclosing an incalculable number of islands. A river twice the size of the Coppermine falls into the sea in N lat. 68° 2', W long. 104° 15'. A strait 10 m. wide at either extremity, but contracting to 3 m. in the centre, appeared to separate the main from Boothia Felix. After this expedition, it remained, in order to complete the northern configuration of America, to trace the space between Dease and Simpson's last limit in N lat. 68° 28' 27", and W long. 93° 7', and the NW boundary of Melville peninsula on the one side, and the SW boundary of Boothia Felix on the other. This was accomplished in 1846 by another expedition despatched by the Hudson's Bay company, under the charge of Dr. John Rae. On the 23d of July they crossed the mouth of the Wager river, and on the 27th arrived on the coast at the bottom of the gulf of Boothia in 67° 13' N, W long. 87° 30'. On the 3d of Aug. they rounded a high bluff cape in 67° 28' N, W long. 87° 40', to which they gave the name of Lady Pelly's cape. Finding it expedient to winter here, they constructed a dwelling which they named Fort Hope, in N lat. 66° 32' 16", W long. 86° 55' 51"; and on the 7th of April, 1847, they again started on their explorations. On the 7th, when 2 m. from the sea, "we struck," says Dr. Rae, "across land to the westward, and built our snow-house on a small lake 4 m. from Point Hargrave. A strong breeze of head-wind, with thick snowdrift, impeded our progress on the 8th, but we nevertheless advanced 7 m. beyond Cape Lady Pelly before encamping. About mid-day on the 10th we arrived opposite a rounded point which was named Cape Weynton. Our course now lay across a bay about 6 m. deep and 10 wide, which received the name of Colville, in honour of the dep.-govr. of the company. The N point of the bay, which we reached the following forenoon, was called Beaufort, after the learned and scientific hydrographer to the Admiralty. Our next encampment was in Keith bay, in N lat. 68° 17', W long. 88° 22'. The coast here took a sharp turn to the eastward, and our Esquimaux companion informed me, that by crossing overland, in a NW direction, to a large bay which he had formerly visited, we should shorten our distance considerably. I decided on adopting the plan proposed, and left the coast on the morning of the 12th. On the 15th, which was very stormy, with a temperature of 20° below zero, we arrived at the steep mud banks of the bay spoken of by our guide, and called by him Ak-ku-li-guwiak. Early in the morning of the 17th, I set out in company with two of the men, for the purpose of following the coast to some point surveyed by Sir John Ross, as I now felt confident that that veteran discoverer was correct in his opinion as to Boothia Felix being part of the American continent. We directed our course to the furthest visible land, which bore NW-true. The weather was beautiful but cold; and the ice being smooth, a brisk walk of 17 m. brought us to the point towards which we had been proceeding, in time to obtain a meridian observation of the sun. Cape Berens is situated in N lat. 69° 4′ 12", and W long. 90° 35'. It is formed entirely of granite, partially covered with moss. Thirteen m. beyond this we arrived at two narrow points in the small bay. The shore still trended to the NW, and we had not travelled more than 4 leagues on the 18th, when the coast took a sharp turn to the eastward. We had been tracing the W side of a deep inlet, which was named Halkett, after one of the members of your hon, board. As we were now near the latitude and longitude of Lord Mayor's bay of Sir John Ross, I struck across land nearly in a N direction; and at noon, when passing over a considerable lake, the lat. 69° 26′ 1" N was observed. Advancing 3 m. beyond this we reached another lake; and, as there was as yet no appearance of the sea, I ordered my companions to build a snow-hut and search for fuel whilst I went to look for the coast. A walk of 20 minutes brought me to an inlet not more than a quarter of a mile wide: this I traced to the westward for 3 m., when my course was again obstructed by land. Ascending some high rocks, from which a good view could be obtained, I thought I could distinguish rough ice in the desired direction. With renewed hopes I set out at a rapid pace, plunging among deep snow, scrambling over rocks and through rough ice, until I gained some rising ground close to the beach. From the spot where I now stood, as far as the eye could see to the NW, lay a large extent of ice-covered sea, studded with innumerable islands. Lord Mayor's bay was before me, and the islands were those named by Sir John Ross, 'the Sons of the Clergy of the Church of Scotland.' The isthmus which connects the land to the northward with Boothia Felix, is only 1 m. broad, and, to judge by the number of stone marks set up on it, appears to be a favourite resort of the natives. Its latitude is N 69° 31', W long. by account, 91° 29' 30". Having still to trace the W shore of Melville peninsula, I started for this purpose on the even- Ing of the 13th of May, intending to travel by night with a chosen party of four men. Our course to the sea was nearly due N, through a chain of lakes, and on the 16th we built our snow-hut on Cape Thomas Simpson, in N lat. 67° 19' 14", W long. 87°, a rocky point which I had visited last autumn in the boat. We left our snow-hut on the evening of the 16th. We advanced only 12 m. the first night. On the 17th we crossed a bay 18 m. wide, and encamped at its N point, opposite to which, and within 2 m. of the shore, there is a large island, which was honoured with the name of his Royal Highness the Prince of Wales. A small island to the S of this was called Sabine island. The general trending of the coast was now NNE; near the shore the banks being high and steep. On the 20th we were detained 24 hours by stormy weather at Cape Lady Simpson, a long point in N lat. 68° 10', W long. 85° 53'. We rounded Selkirk bay (called after the noble earl of that name) on the 21st; and after passing a number of small points and bays, we encamped on what at first appeared to be a part of the main land, but which was afterwards found to be an island. Our snow-house on the 25th was built in N lat. 68° 48', W long. 85° 4', near a small stream, frozen (like all others we had passed) to the bottom. Our course on the following night lay to the W of N, the coast being high and rocky, and indented with numerous inlets. After accomplishing 20 m. in a straight line we encamped. Our provisions being nearly exhausted, I could proceed only half a day's journey further northward, being obliged to return the same night to our present quarters. Leaving one of the men, I set out with the other: the snow fell fast, and the walking was extremely fatiguing. After advancing 10 m. the land turned sharp to the eastward; but as the weather was thick I could not see how far it trended in this direction. When we had waited here nearly an hour, the sky cleared up, and I discovered that we were on the S shore of a considerable bay, and could trace the coast to the northwards for about 12 m. beyond it. To the most distant visible point in N lat. 69° 42', W long. 85° 8', I gave the name of Cape Ellice; the land where we stood was called Cape Crozier, and the intervening bay received the name of Parry bay. Finding it hopeless to attempt reaching the Strait of the Fury and Hecla, from which Cape Ellice could only be a few miles distant, we retraced our steps. At 8.20 on the morning of the 9th of June we arrived at Fort Hope, all well, having been absent 27 days." Between 1821 and 1823, Captain Parry sailed up Hudson's strait, and Fox channel, and passing through the strait betwixt Melville peninsula on the S and Cockburn island to the N,-on which he bestowed the name of the Fury and Hecla strait, after his vessels,-entered the gulf of Boothia. The connexion of Boothia Felix with the main having been ascertained, it yet remains to trace the outline of its west coast to complete the outline of the American continent. Having thus sketched what is known of the northern outline of America, we shall continue our survey of the coast from Hudson's bay, southwards, along the Atlantic. Hudson's bay, a mediterranean or inland sea larger than the Baltic, having a superficies of 300,000 sq. m., runs deep into the mass of the American continent, stretching about 800 m. from N to S, between 51° and 64° N, and 600 m. from E to W, from 78° to 95° W long.; while the southern portion of it, named James's bay, penetrates about 260 m. further south. The shores of this great inland sea are rocky and barren, and it is open for navigation only a few months in the year. It communicates with the Atlantic by Hudson's strait, which is 500 m. in length, and at the narrowest part 80 m. in width. Hudson's bay and strait, with the Atlantic, and the gulf of St. Lawrence, bound on three sides the extensive peninsula of Labrador, of which the extreme west point is Cape Charles, to the S of which the straits of Belle Isle isolate Newfoundland on the N, and form the northern embouchure of the gulf of St. Lawrence. This gulf, an expansion of the estuary of the river of that name, stretches along the southern coast of the peninsula of Labrador, and the northern shores of New Brunswick, Nova Scotia, and the island of Cape Breton, to the strait which separates the latter from Newfoundland, and which forms the principal although unnamed opening of the gulf. On the E it is enclosed by the great island of Newfoundland. The narrow strait of Canso, which separates Cape Breton from the adjacent peninsula of Nova Scotia, forms the third communication of the gulf of St. Lawrence with the Atlantic. Its expansion forms Chedabucto bay; at the southern extremity of which Cape Canso is situated. To the W of Cape Breton, in the gulf of St. Lawrence, is Prince Edward's island, and further N the island of Anticosti. Between New Brunswick and Nova Scotia lies the bay of Fundy, 180 m. in length, and 33 in breadth, containing an area of 6,000 sq. m. Its shores are high and rocky, and its navigation is rendered difficult by the prevalence of fogs, and the rapidity of its tides, which frequently rise to the height of 70 ft. At the mouth of the bay of Fundy is Passamaquoddy bay, an indentation between New Brunswick and Maine. Following the coast-line southwards are Penobscot bay in Maine; Boston bay; Massachusetts or Cape Cod bay, enclosed by the narrow semicircular promontory forming Cape Cod; Narraganset bay, and Rhode island, off the coast of the state which derives its name from the latter; Long island sound, 120 m. in length, and varying from 2 m. to 20 m. in breadth, which separates the island of the same name from the state of Connecticut, and terminates, by a narrow strait called Hell's gates, in New York harbour; Sandy Hook, the SE extremity of New York bay; Delaware bay, an expansion of the estuary of the river of the same name; Chesapeake bay, which forms the estuary of the Susquehanna and several other large rivers, and enters the Atlantic by a mouth 14 m. in width, between Capes Charles and Henry, commonly called the Capes of Virginia. The length of this last bay is about 180 m., and its breadth from 7 to 25; it affords safe and easy navigation to vessels of the largest dimensions, and its numerous indentations afford excellent harbourage. Albemarle and Pimlico sounds consist of a channel enclosed by a series of narrow sandy ridges, on the coast of N Carolina, of which Roanoke island forms the division; the former is about 60 m. in length, and from 4 m. to 15 m. in width, and communicates with the Atlantic by the New Inlet; the latter extends southwards about 100 m., with a breadth varying from 8 to 25 m. Its westerly expanse is formed by the estuaries of the Pimlico and Neuse rivers. It is connected with the ocean by Occacock and Old Topsail inlets, the latter at the southern extremity of Cove sound. Between Cape Hatteras, which forms the most westerly point of N Carolina, and Cape Look-out, is Raleigh's bay; and further S, Onslow bay; separated from which, by a continuation of very narrow sandbanks, is a long and narrow channel terminating at Cape Fear, and navigable by vessels of small dimensions. Long bay, extending from Cape Fear to Winyaw point, Cape Romain, St. Helena sound, Port Royal entrance, on the coast of S Carolina, Wassaw inlet, Oscabaw, St. Catherine's and Sapalo islands and sounds, Doby inlet, Alatamaha, St. Simon, St. Andrew's, and Cumberland sounds, on the coast of Georgia; Cape Canaveral, formed by a projection of the series of narrow ridges which runs along a considerable distance of the eastern coast of the peninsula of Florida; Cape Florida further to the south; Tavernier's key, and Cape Sable, terminate the Atlantic coast-line of the N American portion of the Western hemisphere. The gulf of Mexico, a vast mediterranean sea, 1,100 m. from E to W, and upwards of 700 from N to S, forming the NW division of the great Columbian sea, is bounded on the NE and N by the coasts of Florida, Alabama, and Louisiana; on the NW by Texas; by the Mexican states of Tamaul and Vera Cruz on the W and SW; and by Tabasco and Yucatan on the S and SE. Its coasts are indented with numerous bays, of which the principal belonging to the United States are the bay of Florida, extending from Cape Sable to the Mississippi, and including the minor bays of Chatham, Gallivan, Carlos, Charlotte harbour, Hillsboro or Espiritu Santo Vacasausa, and Santa Fe on the E; and those of Appalachia, Apalachicola, St. Joseph, St. Andrew, Chotahatchu, Pensacola, Mobile, Pascagoula, and Borgue, which communicates by a narrow channel with the lake of Pontchartrain and Chandeleurs on the N, and to the E of the promontory forming the extremity of the delta of the Mississippi; and Plaguemine bay, Atchafalaya, Cote-Blanche, and Vermilion, on the S coast of Louisiana, Galveston bay in Texas, Espiritu Santo, an indentation of one of the narrow channels with which the greater extent of the Texan coast is lined, Tampico or Tamiagua, Vera Cruz, Coazacualco, Terminos, and Campeachy, on the Mexican or western coast of the gulf. Few of these bays afford good harbourage; and, excepting the Mississippi, the rivers which fall into the gulf of Mexico are rendered, by the bars which obstruct their entrances, inaccessible to vessels of heavy burthen. With the exception of the reefs or rocky islets of Florida and Yucatan, and the sandy islands or shoals with which its shores are studded, the area of this gulf is free from obstructions. The principal difficulties of its navigation arise from the force and complexity of its currents. Its waters are remarkable for their high temperature, exceeding that of the ocean, in the same latitude, by nearly 10°. Its entrance, formed by the peninsulas of Florida and Yucatan, is divided by the NW extremity of the island of Cuba into two embouchures,—the gulf-stream, which flows northward through the gulf of Florida or New Bahama channel, and the channel of Yucatan on the S, which connects it with the Caribbean sea or sea of the Antilles. This sea, which forms the south-eastern division of the Columbian sea, is about 1,400 m. long, and from 350 m. to 650 m. in breadth, stretching from the coast of Yucatan all along the northern shores of Central and S America to the northern extremity of British Guayana. It is separated from the Atlantic on the N by the great Columbian archipelago, commonly called the West Indies, consisting of the various groups of the Bahamas or Lucayos, and the Greater and Lesser Antilles, which extend in a continuous chain from the peninsula of Florida to the shores of the southern continent. The principal bays belonging to the Caribbean sea are Honduras bay, extending from the NE extremity of the peninsula of Yucatan to Cape Gracios or Dios, on the NE point of Guatemala; Mosquito bay, on the SW coast of the same province; the gulf of Darien, on the E of the isthmus of that name; the gulf of Maracaybo or Venezuela, a large and deep inlet, east from Point Gallinas, connected by a narrow navigable channel with the lake of the same name, and enclosed on the E by the peninsula of Paraguana; the gulf of Paria, a shallow but wellsheltered inlet, 100 m. from E to W, and about 50 m. in breadth, enclosed between the island of Trinidad and the Columbian main. On the NE of Brazil is the great gulf formed by the confluence of the estuaries of the Amazon and the Rio Para, enclosing the island of Marajo, and presenting the greatest indentation on the Atlantic coast of the southern continent. Further S are Capes St. Roque and St. Augustine, the most easterly points of S America; Bahia di Todos os Santos or All Saints' bay, in the province of Bahia, enclosed to the S by the island of Itaparica, and affording safe and commodious harbourage; the bay of St. Salvador, on the E coast of Rio Janeiro; Cape Frio, at the SE extremity of the province; and farther W, the bay of Rio Janeiro and Grande island. Southwards are the island of St. Catherina; the Lago dos Patos, connected with the Atlantic by the narrow channel of the Barro do Rio Grande de St. Pedro; the Rio de la Plata, an immense estuary formed by the confluence of the rivers Araguay and Parana, 170 m. wide at its entrance, between Capes St. Maria on the N, and St. Antonio on the S; Port Belgrano or Bahia Blanca, a small inlet on the southern coast of Buenos Ayres; the gulf of San Matias; the peninsula of St. Josef, formed by the bay or port of the same name on the N, and by the Bahia Nuovo on the S; Bahia de los Camerones de St. Jorje, extending to Cape Three Points, Puerto Descado, Bahia Grande, and Cape Virgins, terminating the Atlantic coast-line. Its southern extremity is separated from the archipelago of Terra del Fuego by the straits of Magellan or Magalhaens, which forms a navigable channel about 300 m. in length, and varying in breadth from 11 m. to 40 m. between the Atlantic and Pacific oceans. Between the western promontory of Eastern Terra del Fuego and Staten island is Le Maire strait; and on a small island at the south of the archipelago, Cape Horn, forming the most southerly point of America. The group of the Falkland isles, forming one of the most important in the antarctic archipelago, lies to the NE of the Terra del Fuegan islands. The western shore of Patagonia presents numerous sinuosities, studded with islands of various dimensions, forming the Patagonian archipelago. Messier channel separates Wellington island from the main land; and to the N is the gulf of Penas, and Tres Montes cape and peninsula. Further N is the Chonos archipelago, with the gulf of the same name, and that of Ancudo, the latter of which separates the island of Chiloe from the provinces of Patagonia and Chili; Conception bay, Cape Malabrigo, the most westerly point of the continent; the gulf of Guayaquil, between Cape Blanco and Point St. Elena, Cape St. Francisco, and Cape Guascama, the southern extremity of the bay of Choco, form the principal indentations of the southern peninsula of America. The bay of Panama, terminating in Point Mala, forms, with the minor gulfs of St. Miguel on the E, and of Parita on the W, the southern curve of the isthmus of the same name; and fills an expanse of about 130 m. in diameter. Towards the E of the bay is the archipelago de las Perlas, and on the NW are the rocky islands forming the harbour of Panama. Beyond the Morro de Puercos is the bay of Montijo, and further to the NW are the gulfs of Nicoya, Papagayo, Conchagua or Fonseca, and Tehuantepec; Cape Corrientes, in the province of Xalisco, and the gulf of California or sea of Cortes, 700 m. in length, and from 40 to 150 in breadth, formed by the peninsula of California,—a long narrow neck of land, terminating at Cape St. Lucas. A range of rocky islands stretches along the western coast of the gulf, and towards the NE is the island of Tuberon. On the NW coast of Mexico is the bay or port of St. Francisco, which, extending inland about 50 m., forms the estuary of several considerable rivers, and affords excellent harbourage. Stretching from Cape Flattery is the channel—consisting of the supposed strait of Juan de Fuca, the gulf of Georgia, and Queen Charlotte's sound-which separates the great island of Quadra and Vancouver, in the archipelago of New Georgia, from the Columbian coast, and on the western side of the island is Nootka sound. Further north the outline of the coast presents numerous sinuosities, filled with ranges of islands and island groups of various dimensions, extending to a considerable distance from the coast, forming the archipelagos of New Hanover, New Cornwall, and New Norfolk. That tract of country which is commonly called the North-west coast of America, comprehends the whole line of coast from 48° 55' N, and 125° W long., as far as 65° 46' N and 169° 15' W. Previous to the discoveries of the illustrious Cook, this extensive range of coast had not been explored. Some faint gleams of light, indeed, were thrown on this hitherto obscure subject by the Russians, who, having gradually extended their settlements to the E and NE extremities of the Asiatic continent, discovered the great chain of the Aleutian islands, stretching from Kamtschatka to Cape Alaska—the western extremity of the peninsula of that name—and connecting the two continents. The straits separating the two continents were discovered by Behring, a Dane, employed by the Russian court, on a similar plan of discovery with that of Cook, in 1728. Through these straits he sailed as far as N lat. 67° 18', without discovering the approximation of the American and Asiatic continents. The whole extent of this coast north of the island of Fuca, is so studded with islands, and so broken with sounds, bays, and inlets, as to present no appearance of continuity. Its extent, from the entrance of Fuca to the head of Cook's inlet, is 1,200 m. in direct distance. Thence the coast trends SW to Cape Alaska, and 1,200 miles E of Kamtschatka. The line of coast in this direction extends 800 m. From Cape Alaska to the head of Bristol bay, the coast turns to NE for a space of 350 m. It then bends NW for 150 m.; and then runs almost due N as far as the head of Norton-sound, 400 m. in direct distance. Thence its general direction is NW to Cape Prince of Wales, the western extremity of North America, 160 m. further in direct distance; making a total of 3,060 miles, exclusive of the windings of the coast. General Aspect and Features.] When we cast our eyes on the great American continent, the first thing which strikes us, besides its extraordinary magnitude as a whole, is the large forms in which Nature has cast its different physical features. Its rivers are large and rapid beyond those of the ancient continents; and after falling into the ocean, they give rise to currents which are perceptible at a very great distance from their mouths. The Maranon, the Orinoco, and the Plata, in South America, and the Mississippi, and St. Lawrence, in North America, are all conspicuous for the width of the channels in which they flow, and the prodigious mass of waters which they each contribute to the ocean. Then there is no chain of mountains on this side of the globe—the Himalaya excepted—which, in extent and altitude, can be compared with the Andes of South America: the Alps themselves dwindle into insignificance in the comparison. The plains likewise of the New world are as extensive and beautiful as its mountains are elevated and grand. In some places, and at certain seasons of the year, the eye of the traveller in vain attempts to scan the farthest verge of the plain which stretches its monotonous expanse before him like some vast ocean; in other quarters, the whole country is one wide savannah, teeming with vegetable life, and clothed in the fairest hues of creation. Its lakes are equally remarkable. In North America there is a chain of lakes, each of them an inland sea in magnitude; and the lakes even of a third class, in America, equal if they do not excel any feature of this kind of which the Old world can boast. The great leading features in the structure of the New world are: 1st. The continuous belt of high mountains and plateaus traversing the western side of the continent at a short distance from the coast, from Behring's straits to Terra del Fuego, and forming the most uninterrupted extent of primitive mountains known. Their northern portion, consisting of the Rocky mountains, within the limits of the United States, and the Chippewayan range in the Northwest territory, appears to be chiefly granitic; while in the Cordilleras of Mexico, and the Andes of South America, the primitive strata are covered with immense accumulations of transition-porphyries, and lavas, the produce of numerous volcanoes, many of which yet remain in constant activity. 2dly. The wide expanse of low and generally level country which succeeds immediately to the west of the abovementioned zone of mountains; and through which, in both hemispheres, flow some of the most magnificent rivers in the world. This region consists of immense deposits of newer rocks, over which is strewed an alluvial formation, with intermingled rolled masses of rock. 3dly. The chain of mountains, of lower elevation and inferior continuity, which forms the eastern boundary to the low country, and whose principal masses and highest points are composed of granite. 4thly. The magnificent inland collections of water. 5thly. The clusters of islands occupying the seas between North and South America, and which are, almost without exception, of a volcanic origin. The geological character of America partakes of the simplicity observable in her mountain-ranges, which obey very uniform laws of arrangement, and are in a great measure free from those interruptions which occur in Europe, arising out of numerous chains, whose irregular and often contradictory structure-as geologists would say-it is frequently difficult to reconcile or explain. The two continents, however, agree in the prevailing primitive character of their northern extremities, and in the occurrence of volcanoes about their equatorial and southern regions; and an investigation of their geological relations affords no ground for the opinion that the New world—as it is chronologically called—is of more recent origin than the Old. According to Humboldt all the great elevations of the New world stand connected with that prodigious chain which, following the direction of the western DDD DDD DDD DDD DDD DDD DDD DDD DDD DD coast, stretches, under different names, and with considerable interruptions, from the northern extremity of the continent to the southern, over a space of 10,000 m. in length. This mountain-system vastly exceeds its only rival the Himalaya in length, and never, like it, loses its mountain-character in the wide level expanse of an elevated plateau, or is interrupted by broad valleys and the intersection of mighty streams. Unlike its Asiatic rival, moreover, it rises at once from near sea-level on both sides of the range. It may be considered as formed of three sections: 1st. The Andes, properly so called, extending from Cape Horn to the isthmus of Panama; 2d, the Central American system, extending from Panama to the peninsula of Tehuantepec; and 3d. the North American chain extending from the Mexican plateau to Behring's straits. System of the Andes.] The Andes Proper may be subdivided into four sections. The 1st., forming the southern section of the system, and extending from the southern extremity of the continent to the 44th southern parallel, may be distinguished as the Patagonian Andes, sometimes called the Sierra Nevada de los Andes; and is that portion of the system which is least known to geographers. The 2d. section, extending from the 44th to the 20th southern parallel, is the Andes of Chili and Potosi. The 3d. is the Peruvian Andes, extending from the 20th parallel to the plateau of Almaguer under N lat. 1° 50', and sometimes called the Royal cordillera or Grand cordillera of Peru. The 4th section is the Cordilleras of New Granada. Till very lately none of the peaks of this immense range had been measured but those in the presidency of Quito; and it was the generally received opinion that the Andes attained their greatest height in the vicinity of Quito. This, however, has eventually proved to have been but unauthorized conjecture, as from Cape Horn all the way to the peak of Tolima—a line of 60 degrees of a great circle—the number of summits actually measured was very small; and there was no reason, a priori, why those measured should have been esteemed the highest, merely because they were under the equatorial line. But the idea was long pertinaciously and keenly maintained that the highest summits of all mountain-ranges must necessarily be under the equator; and that all mountain-chains must diminish in height as they recede from that line. This theory was completely disproved in the case of the Himalaya range, and the mountains of Tibet, which are from 28 to 30 degrees N of that line; and has again been disproved by the labours of our countryman, Mr. Pentland, who, in 1826 and 1827, explored the Andes of Titicaca in Upper Peru, in the modern republic of Bolivia, and measured the summits trigonometrically, and all the accessible heights by the barometer. According to Mr. Pentland, the great chain of the Andes, which forms a single and continuous ridge from the southern extremity of the American continent to the neighbourhood of the tropic of Capricorn, separates into two great ridges near the city of Potosi, in S lat. 19° 35'; and these, after enclosing a vast alpine plain raised 12,000 to 13,000 ft. above the level of the sea, reunite in about 14° S, where they form the Andes of Vilcanote and Cusco. The western of these ridges runs parallel to the shores of the Pacific, and is called the cordillera of the Coast; the eastern, or that of the interior, is called the Cordillera Real. The intermediate plain is the basin of the celebrated lake Titicaca, the physical features of which are scarcely less extraordinary than its history is interesting. Generally speaking, the western cordillera is the most elevated, attaining, at many points, an absolute height of from 22,000 to 24,000 ft.; while the eastern cordillera, between the latitudes of 19° and 16° 40' S, nowhere exceeds 17,000. In the latter parallel, however, the gigantic Illimani springs to the height of 24,200 ft.; and north of it, several other elevated points even surpass the height of the western ridge. The most elevated is the Nevado de Sorata, in S lat. 16° 10′, the height of which is 25,250 ft. In general shape and character, the two ridges also differ. The heights in the western are chiefly dome or bellshaped; those in the eastern are peaked, giving the range generally a serrated form. The descent of both, east and west, is rapid; but that of the western cordillera, into the basin of Titicaca, is less so than that of the eastern. The breadth of the former is about 100 m.; that of the latter it is less easy to determine, in consequence of its throwing out many lateral chains on its eastern side, the length of which may be considered portions of the breadth of the main ridge. Excluding these, however, this may be estimated at from 35 m. where narrowest (17° 58' S), to above 70 m. where widest (16° 50' S). The entire width of the two ridges, including that also of the basin of Titicaca, varies from 200 to 300 m., exclusive of the projecting chains; including them it approaches 500 m.; and the length of this portion of the Andean chain, bounded by the 14th and 20th southern parallels, is nearly 400 miles. In the environs of Popayan, the Andes chain exhibits a trifurcation in which the Sierra de la Suma Paz, which is the eastern chain, may be regarded as the continuation of the principal chain. It traverses Columbia from SW to NE, passing to the E of Bogota and Truxillo, to the N of Valencia, and to the S of New Barcelona and Cumana. The central chain, called Quindiu, separates the valley of the Magdalena from that of the Cauca. The western chain, called Choco, is greatly inferior in elevation to either of the other two, and sinks down to the level of the plain beneath the gulf of Cupica and the Rio Napipi. From the Andes of Chili, a considerable branch, known as the Sierra du Tucuman, runs off to the SE, where it sinks gradually into the llanos of Tucuman. The isolated Sierra Nevada de Santa Marta in the department of Magdalena may be regarded as a detached portion of the principal chain of the cordilleras of New Granada. The system of La Parime, or the highlands of Guayana, between the plains of the Lower Orinoco and those of the Rio Negro and the Amazon, is an irregularly grouped mass of mountains between the parallels of 3° and 8° N, and the meridians of 58° and 68° W. Its highest summits are situated on the southernmost chain, at the foot of which the plains of the Cassiquiari and the Rio Negro begin. Here the Pic de Dinda attains an elevation of 8,212 ft.; and the Maravaco probably reaches 10,500 ft. The Brazilian mountains present three principal chains, all of which rise with different degrees of inclination from S to N, and of which the principal seems to be the Serra do Espinhaço, which is probably the most elevated, though not the longest. It stretches from the right bank of the San Francisco to the Uraguay, or from the 10th to the 28th parallel. Its highest summits occur in the prov. of Minas Geraes. The longest chain is the Serra dos Vertentes, which describes an immense semicircle from the southern frontier of the prov. of Seara to the western extremity of the prov. of Matto Grosso. The Serra Borborema, a branch of the latter chain, terminates in Cape San Roque. Central American system.] Long before the Andes have reached the isthmus of Panama, they have dwindled into inconsiderable hills; and in Veragua, the Sierra de Canatagua is said to run in an opposite direction to the Andes, as if to interrupt the extension of the chain into North America. It is probable, however, that the general direction of this chain will be found to follow that of the narrow isthmus on which it rises. Under the parallel of 9° 25' N the group of Costa Rica rises into a lofty plateau which falls suddenly on the E towards the Caribbean sea, and is united to another chain running towards the lake of Nicaragua.-The Honduras and Nicaragua group consist of mountains whose connexion is very imperfectly known.—The Guatemala chain running along the western coast of Central America, towards the bay of Tehuantepec, is distinguished by its numerous volcanic summits. Its culminating point appears to be the volcano of Agua, whose alt. Hall has estimated at 14,760 ft. The volcano of Soconusco in N lat. 15° 59' terminates the volcanic series of the Guatemala mountains, which enter Mexico under the designation of the Cordillera de Oaxaca, and as they advance towards the NW assume that of the Cordillera de Mexico. North American system.] In the Mexican province of Oaxaca, the chain occupies the centre of the isthmus, between the sources of the Chimalapa and the Huasecualco. Of this part of the chain, the Cerro de Sinpualtepec is said to be so elevated that both the Atlantic and Pacific are visible from it: an extent of visual horizon indicating an elevation of 7,700 ft. From 18½° to 21° N lat. the cordillera stretches from S to N, and approaches the Atlantic. In the centre of the table-land, an elevated chain separates the valley of Mexico from the intendancy of Pueblo de Los Angelos. Of this chain, the most elevated summits are the Popoca-tepetl, a volcano covered with perpetual snow, its elevation being 17,735 ft. above sea-level, and the Istac-cihuatl, having an alt. of 15,700 ft., according to Humboldt, who took its height, as well as that of the former mountain, geometrically. According to Mr. Sonninschmide, a German mineralogist, who measured its height by the barometer, its elevation was 14,830 ft., or 870 ft. lower than Humboldt's estimate. The Nevado de Toluca, another colossal summit, 22 m. SW of Mexico, is elevated 15,156 ft. above sea-level, though it is only 6,299 ft. above the plain whence it springs. The Cerro de Axusco, 12 m. direct distance SSW from Mexico, has 12,052 ft. of absolute height, and is 4,596 ft. above the level of Tezcuco. The mean elevation of the whole range of which these summits make a part, and which surround as with a circular wall the valley of Mexico, is, according to Humboldt, 9,842 ft. The eastern chain which runs N and S along the eastern declivity of the table-land, towards the gulf of Mexico, presents the elevated summits of the Citlaltepetl, -called Orizava or Orizaba in the maps, -and Nauhcampa-tepetl. The absolute height of the former is 17,387 ft., and of the latter 13,514 ft., above the level of the sea, according to Humboldt. From the north-eastern part of the intendancy of Mexico, the chain assumes the name of Sierra Madre, and runs NW to Guanaxuato. North of this city, it becomes of an extraordinary breadth,-dividing into three branches, of which the most eastern runs in the direction of Charcas and Catorce, and is lost in the province of New Leon. The western branch proceeds to the N of Guadalajara, and passing to the E of Culiacan and Arispe, as far N as the source of the Rio Gila, forms in its progress the mountains of Primeria Alta, celebrated for the gold washed down their sides. This western branch is denominated the ridge of Topia by Spanish writers; and from its western side many lateral chains stretch SW to the gulf of California. The middle branch of the Sierra Madre, which may be viewed as the central chain of the Mexican Alps, occupies the whole extent of the inrango and New Biscay, as far as the Sierras de los Mimbros to the W of the Rio del Norte. Thence it traverses New Mexico, till it meets with the Topian ridge on the W, and on the E with a snowy ridge that commences on the E of the Rio del Norte, in 31° N lat., and running N and then NW, forms the boundary in that quarter between New Mexico and Louisiana. This termination of the Sierra Madre, in 41° N lat., divides the rivers which flow towards the Pacific from those which discharge themselves into the Atlantic ocean. From this point, where the various branches meet, they again spread out to a great breadth, under the appellation of the Stony or Rocky mountains. Another snowy range extends NW and SE between the lower courses of the Colorado and the Buenaventura rivers, running parallel with the E coast of the gulf of California; and another chain, called the Californian mountains, commences near the SE point of the peninsula of that name, and runs in a NW course through the middle of the peninsula, and along the coast of the Pacific, as far as the mouth of the Columbia, -an extent of more than 1,500 m. The prevailing geological feature of the Mexican mountain-system is porphyritic rock. The central plateau of Anahuac presents an enormous porphyritic dyke, distinguished from the European porphyry by the constant presence of hornblende and the absence of quartz. Granite appears at Acapulco and in Oaxaca. Balbi proposes to designate the mountain-ranges now described, from the cordillera of Veragua to the Rocky mountains, the Missouri-Mexican system. The longitudinal extent of the Rocky mountainchain is immense. It certainly reaches the 60th northern parallel, and perhaps only terminates on the shores of the Arctic ocean. Its mean breadth is 200 The perpetual snows and innumerable fountains of this enormous range, from the 38th to the 48th m. parallel, feed with never-failing supplies, the vast Missouri and its tributary host of powerful streams. In endeavouring to explore the sources of the Red and Arkansaw rivers, Captain Pike and his whole party were completely bewildered amidst snows, torrents, and precipices. The cold was so intense, that several of the party had their limbs frostbitten, and were abandoned to their fate by Pike and his surviving companions. In a lateral ridge separating the valley of the Arkansaw from that of the Platte, in N lat. 41°, is a remarkable peak, called the Great White mountain, which formed the boundary of their explorations to the NW. The alt. of this peak was taken on the base of a mile by Pike, and found to be 10,581 ft. above the level of the meadow at its base; and the height of this latter was estimated at 8,000 ft. above the level of the sea; giving in all, 18,581 ft. of absolute elevation, or 6,000 ft. higher than the peak of Teneriffe, by Humboldt's measurement; and 2,852 ft. short of that of Chimborazo, admitting the elevation of this last to be 21,472 ft. Captain Pike and his companions never lost sight of this tremendous peak, unless in a valley, for the space of ten weeks wandering amongst the mountains. The elevation of the sources of the Missouri is only matter of conjecture. The level of the river, where they left their canoes, could not be less than 6,000 ft. above the sea; but how high the mountains rose above this point the narrative does not inform us. The central chain, as usual, is marked in Pike's map as the highest, and as covered with snow during the whole year. The latitude is between 45 and 47 N. Between these parallels, in Europe, the lower limit of perpetual congelation is fixed at from 9,000 to 10,000 ft. above the level of the sea; and it can hardly be supposed that the summits of this range were less than 8,500 or 9,000 ft. high, making a reasonable allowance for the greater coldtendancy of Zacatecas, and stretches through Du- ness of the American continent. A great number of lateral ranges project to the SE, E, and NE of the main range. Where the Missouri enters the plains, is the most eastern projection; and from where the Jaune leaves the snowy range, a lateral range runs off more than 200 m. SE, which is intersected by the Bighorn river. As these mountains have not yet been explored by the eye of geological science, it is impossible to say any thing respecting their component parts; but they seem to be chiefly granitic. No volcanoes have yet been discovered amongst them. The mountains E of the Mississippi chiefly belong to what is called the Alleghany system, of which the eastern chain is known as the Blue mountains; and the western chain as the Cumberland mountains. The general direction of the chains composing this system is from NE to SW, and they stretch between the 35th and 41st parallels. We may regard as dependencies of this system the range of mountains running along the northern shores of the gulf of St. Lawrence; the low range traversing the whole of Lower Canada and Labrador; the range between the gulf of St. Lawrence and Hudson's bay; and the Wisconsin hills between Lake Michigan and the Mississippi. Little is known of the orography of the regions within the Arctic circle; but the direction of the mountain-chains in these regions appears to be from SE to NW.—The name Arctic Highlands has been given to the elevated region extending from the shore of Hudson's bay westward to the mouth of the Mackenzie river. Plateaux.] The surface of the American continent presents a number of remarkable plateaux, or elevated plains, whose boundaries and extent are defined by mountain-ridges. Of these the Peruvian plateau, which embraces all the high lands of Lower Peru, Bolivia, and the Rio de la Plata provinces, and stretches from the 6th to the 26th southern parallel, has an altitude varying from 3,800 ft. to 8,900 ft. The basin of the celebrated lake of Titicaca, 150 geographical miles in length by about 50 in breadth, occupies the centre of a plateau elevated on the top of that just described, between 15° 35' and 17° 30' S, and 68° and 72° W.—The Columbian plateau, which comprises all the highest vallies in Assuay, Ecuador, Cundinamarca, and Boyaca, has an elevation varying from 5,200 ft. to 9,600 ft.—The Brazilian plateau, comprising the upper part of the basins of the San Francisca and Parana, and the most elevated districts in the prov. of Rio de Janeiro, Espiritu Santo, Bahia, Pernambuco, and Piauhy, has an elevation of from 650 ft. to 1,650 ft.—The Central plateau of South America, in which Balbi comprises the vast Brazilian prov. of Matto Grosso, and portions of Goyaz and San Paulo, Paraguay, and the Chaco in the Rio de la Plata confederacy; and the territory of the Chiquetos and Moxos in Bolivia, has an alt. of from 600 ft. to 2,500 ft.—The plateau of Guayana, which embraces the country lying between the Orinoco, the Rio Negro, the Amazon, and the Atlantic, has an alt. of from 2,500 ft. to 5,000 ft.—The plateau of Anahuac or Mexico, extending from Oaxaca to Chihuahua, and comprising all the high land of Guatemala, has an elevation of from 3,800 ft. to 8,800 ft.—The Missouri-Columbian plateau, or Central plateau of North America, comprising the upper portion of the basins of the Missouri, the Saskatchevan, the Mackenzie, the Columbia, the Oregon, the Lewis, the Western Colorado, the Rio del Norte, the Arkansas, the Flat River, and the Yellow-stone river, is elevated from 2,300 ft. to 3,500 ft.—The Alleghanian plateau, comprising the high plains of Georgia, the Carolinas, Tennessee, Virginia, of part of Kentucky, Maryland, Pennsylvania, New Jersey, New York, Massachusetts, Vermont, New Hampshire, Maine, and some parts of Lower Canada, has an elevation of from 1,200 ft. to 3,200 ft. [Balbi.] Plains.] In no other region of the globe do we behold such vast plains as present themselves over the whole extent of the American continent. From the delta of the Mackenzie river on the shores of the Arctic ocean, to that of the Mississippi on the gulf of Mexico, and between the Central chain of the Missouri-Mexican mountain-system on the W, to the Alleghany system on the E, extends a vast plain, the largest in the world, embracing the basins of the Mississippi, the St. Lawrence, the Churchill, the Saskatchevan, the Mackenzie, the Coppermine, and nearly the whole basin of the Missouri, and presenting a superficies which has been estimated at 3,250,000 sq. m. This immense tract of country is divided into two basins by a low water-shed; that on the NE receiving the waters which flow into the Polar sea, Hudson's bay, the great Canadian lakes, the St. Lawrence, and the Atlantic; and the other basin, the waters which fall into the gulf of Mexico. This southern basin contains those vast grassy plains called savannahs or prairies. "The name of savannahs is given to those vast prairies of the Western region which display a boundless ocean of verdure, and deceive the sight by seeming to rise towards the sky, and of which the only inhabitants are immense herds of bisons or buffaloes; also to those plains which skirt the rivers, and are generally inundated in the rainy season. The trees which grow there are of the aquatic species. The Magnolia glauca, the American olive, the Gordonia argentea with its odorous flowers, are seen here, isolated or in groups, while the general surface of the savannah exhibits a long and succulent herbage mixed with plants and shrubs." [M. Brun. When seen from the summits of the Mexican and the Rocky mountains, they seem absolutely boundless to the view. They frequently undulate into gentle swelling lawns, or expand into spacious vallies, in the centre of which is always found a little timber growing on the banks of brooks and rivulets of the finest water. Pike - who viewed them from the summit of the Blue mountains, under the source of the Arkansaw-says: "The unbounded prairie was overhung with clouds, which seemed like the ocean in a storm,—wave piled on wave, and foaming; while the sky over our heads was perfectly clear, and the prospect was truly sublime." It would seem that the view of these unbounded prairies excited sensations in the mind of Pike similar to those with which Timur Bec beheld the steppes of Kapshac, according to his historian. In these vast prairies the soil is dry and sandy; but the moment we approach a stream, the land becomes more humid, and is clothed with small timber. It is probable that these steppes or prairies were never well-wooded; as, from the aridity of the soil, having few water-courses running through it, and these principally dry in summer, no sufficient nourishment is afforded to the growth of timber. In all timbered land, the annual discharge of the leaves, with the continual decay of old trees and branches, creates at once manure and moisture; but in Upper Louisiana, a barren soil, parched and dried up for eight months in the year, presents neither moisture nor nutriment sufficient for the growth of wood. These vast plains of Louisiana, near the upper course of the Arkansaw with its tributary streams, and the head-waters of the Kanzas, and White and Grand Osage rivers, may become in time like the sandy deserts of Africa. "I saw in my route," says Pike, "in various places, tracts of many leagues, where the wind had thrown up the sand in all the fancied forms of the ocean's rolling waves, and on which not a single speck of vegetation appeared." These prairies, from the borders of the Mississippi on the E, to the base of the Mexican Alps on the W, rise with a continually increasing acclivity for many hundred miles, till, at the base of the mountains, they attain an elevation of 8,000 ft., as we are informed by Pike, which is greater than the elevated level of the great desert of Gobi on the NW of China, estimated by Du Halde to be 5,511 ft. above the level of the sea; or of the Karoo or arid desert to the N of the cape of Good Hope, traversed by the Orange river, and the elevation of which is estimated by Colonel Gordon at 6,561 ft. In addition to the aridity of the Louisiana prairies, they are so impregnated with nitre and other salts, as to taint the waters which flow across them. Pike says, that for leagues together, they are covered with saline incrustations; a number of tributary streams descending into the Arkansaw and Kanzas rivers are perfect salines; and beyond the river Platte, the lands are not only destitute of timber, but even of good water, of which there is but a small quantity in the creeks, and even that is brackish. The same saline incrustations pervade the prairies on the Upper Missouri; and the same want of timber and little or no dew with very little rain, continues to the neighbourhood of the mountains. There are no prairie lands N of the Peace river; and no flat country skirting the Rocky mountains beyond Great Slave lake [Richardson].—The second great plain of America is that of the Amazon, which comprises the whole central part of South America, including more than half of Brazil, the southwestern part of Colombia, the eastern part of Peru, and the northern part of Bolivia. Its limits are nearly identical with that of the basin of the Amazon and of the Rio Tocantin. This plain is everywhere fertile in the highest degree, and clothed with immense and impenetrable forests, especially on the banks of the rivers. Humboldt estimates the area of this plain at 2,340,000 sq. m.—The plain of the Rio de la Plata extends between the Andes on the W; the mountains of Brazil on the N; and the Atlantic ocean on the E and S. Humboldt estimates its superficies at 1,215,000 sq. m. A large portion of it is known by the name of the pampas. These are plains which present one uniform expanse of waving grass, uninterrupted either by forest or eminence. They commence 73 m. W of Buenos Ayres; and are in some places parched and barren, in others, fertile, covered with high grass, destitute of trees, and mostly uninhabited. They are the abode of innumerable herds of wild oxen, horses, ostriches, and other animals, which, under the shade of the grass, find protection from the intolerable heat of the sun; and towards the Chilian frontier, guanucas and vacunas roam over them in considerable numbers. Over these pampas, as in the Russian and Tartarian steppes, there are no landmarks for many hundred miles, so that travellers are obliged to pursue their route by the compass. The plain of the Orinoco, or of the Guaviare-Orinoco, as Balbi proposes to call it, embracing the llanos, as they are called, of New Granada and Venezuela, extends from Caqueta to the mouth of the Orinoco, along the Guaviare, the Meta, and the lower course of the Orinoco. Humboldt has estimated its superficies at 261,000 sq. m. The llanos.] "There is something awful, but sad and gloomy," remarks that learned and eloquent traveller, "in the uniform aspect of these steppes. Every thing there seems motionless: seldom does a small cloud, as it crosses the zenith, and announces the approach of the rainy season, cast its shadow on the savannah. I know not whether the first aspect of the llanos excites less astonishment than that of the Andes. Mountainous countries, -whatever may be the absolute elevation of the highest summits,—have an analogous physiognomy; but we accustom ourselves with difficulty to the view of the llanos of Venezuela and Casanare, or the pampas of Buenos Ayres and Chaco, which recall to mind continually, during journeys of 20 or 30 days, the smooth surface of the ocean. I had seen the plains of La Mancha in Spain, and the real steppes which extend from Jutland, through Lunenburg and Westphalia, to Belgium; but the plains of the W and N of Europe present but a feeble image | of the immense llanos of South America: all around us, they seemed to ascend toward the sky; and the vast and profound solitude appeared like an ocean covered with sea-weeds. Owing to the unequal mass of vapours diffused through the atmosphere, and the various temperature of the different strata of air, the horizon was in some parts clear and distinct, in others, undulating, sinuous, and as if striped: the earth was there confounded with the sky. Through the dry fog and strata of vapour, the trunks of palm-trees were discerned at a great distance; and stripped of their foliage and verdant tops, these trunks appeared like the masts of a ship discovered at the horizon. The llanos and the pampas of South America are real steppes. They display a beautiful verdure in the rainy season; but in continued drought assume the aspect of a desert. The grass is then reduced to powder,-the earth cracks,-and the alligator and great serpents remain buried in the dry mud till awakened from their long lethargy by the first showers of spring. These phenomena are observed on barren tracks of 50 or 60 leagues in length, wherever the savannahs are not traversed by rivers; for, on the borders of rivulets, and around little pools of stagnant water, the traveller, even during the period of the great droughts, encounters here and there thickets of mauritia,—a palm the leaves of which, spread out like a fan, preserve a brilliant verdure. The chief characteristic of the savannahs or steppes of South America is, the absolute want of hills and inequalities, the perfect level of every part of the soil. Accordingly, the Spanish conquerors who first penetrated from Coro to the banks of the Apure, did not call them deserts, or savannahs, or meadows, but plains,-llanos. Often, in a space of 30 sq. leagues, there is not an eminence of a foot high; and the resemblance to the surface of the sea strikes the imagination most powerfully where the plains are altogether destitute of palm-trees, and where the mountains of the shore and of the Orinoco are so distant that they cannot be seen, as in the Mesa de Pavones. This equality of surface is still more perfect in the meridian of Calabozo than toward the east, between the Cari, La Villa del Pao, and Nueva Barcelona; but it reigns without interruption from the mouths of the Orinoco to La Villa de Araure and Ospinos, under a parallel of 180 leagues in length; and from San Carlos to the savannahs of Caqueta, on a meridian of 200 leagues. It particularly characterises the New continent, as it does the low steppes of Asia between the Borysthenes and the Wolga, and between the Irtisch and the Obi. The deserts of Central Africa, of Arabia, Syria, and Persia, Cobi, and Casna, on the contrary, present many inequalities,—ranges of hills, ravines without water, and rocks that pierce the sands. The llanas, however, notwithstanding the apparent uniformity of their surface, furnish two kinds of inequalities which will not escape the observation of an attentive traveller. The first is known by the name of bancos: these are real shoals in the basin of the steppes, fractured strata of sandstone or compact limestone, standing four or five feet higher than the rest of the plain. These banks are sometimes 3 or 4 leagues in length; are entirely smooth, with a horizontal surface; and their existence is only perceived by examining their borders. The second species of inequality can be recognised only by geodesical or barometric levellings, or by the course of rivers. It is called mesa; and is composed of small flats, or rather convex eminences, which rise insensibly to the height of a few toises. Such are, in the province of Cumana, to the N of the Villa de la Merced and Candelaria, the mesas of Amana, of Guanipa, and of Jonoro, the direction of which is SW and NE; and which, in spite of their inconsiderable elevation, form a water-shed between the Orinoco and the northern coast of Terra Firma. The convexity of the savannah alone occasions this partition. We here find a divortia aquarum existing as in Poland, where, far from the Carpathian mountains, the plain itself divides the waters between the Baltic and the Black sea. Geographers, who suppose that there must always exist a chain of mountains wherever there is a line of division, have not failed to mark one in their maps, at the sources of the Rio Neveri, the Unare, the Guarapiche, and the Pao. The uniform landscape of the llanos,—the extreme paucity of their inhabitants,—the fatigue of travelling beneath a burning sky and an atmosphere darkened by dust,— the view of that horizon which seems for ever to flee before us,those lonely trunks of palm-trees which have all the same aspect, and which we despair of reaching because they are confounded with other trunks that rise by degrees on the visual horizon,all these causes combined make the steppes appear far greater than they are in reality. The planters who inhabit the southern declivity of the chain of the coast, see the steppes extending toward the S, as far as the eye can reach, like an ocean of verdure. They know, that, from the delta of the Orinoco to the province of Varinas, and thence, by traversing the banks of the Meta, the Guaviare, and the Caguan, they can advance 380 leagues in the plains, first from E to W, and then from NE to SE, beyond the equator, to the foot of the Andes of Pasto: they are likewise acquainted, from the accounts of travellers, with the pampas of Buenos Ayres which also are llanos covered with fine grass, destitute of trees, and filled with oxen and horses become wild; and they suppose, according to the greater part of our maps of America, that this continent has only one chain of mountains,—that of the Andes,—which stretches from S to N; and form a vague idea of the contiguity of all the plains from the Orinoco and the Apure to the Rio de la Plata and the straits of Magellan." Rivers. America is unrivalled by any other portion of the globe in the number and magnitude of the rivers by which it is intersected, and in which | every portion of this vast continent possesses a medium of commercial intercourse in which the wealth of future generations may be confidently predicted. Of these,—the greatest of which fall either directly into the Atlantic or into its gulfs, -are: The St. Lawrence, one of the most remarkable for the volume and depth of its waters, and the immense width of its embouchure. This river, in the commonly limited acceptation of the name, issues from the chain of lakes which lie to the S of Hudson's bay, at a point called the Lake of the Thousand isles; but if the opinion of Balbi, that the immense inland seas of which this river forms the outlet, ought to be regarded as the expansion of the upper waters of the river itself, be adopted, its sources are to be found in the Kamistiquia, or in the St. Louis, which form the principal tributaries of Lake Superior; thence it is to be traced in the strait or falls of St. Mary, 50 m. long, in its descent to Lake Huron; and after receiving the waters of Lake Michigan, by the strait of Mackinac, 40 m. long, in the river St. Clair, which conducts to the small lake of the same name.
