Issuing thence, it is discharged by the Detroit, 25 m. in length, into Lake Erie; from which it discharges itself, by the Niagara and far-famed falls of Niagara, into Lake Ontario. On issuing from the latter lake at Kingston, it takes the name of the Iroquois, and forms the boundary line between Upper Canada and the State of New York; and after intersecting Lower Canada in a NE direction, and forming the wide expanses called Lake St. Francis, Lake St. Louis, and St. Peter's lake, and taking the name of St. Lawrence after passing Montreal, it terminates in the gulf to which it gives its name, at Gaspé point, by a mouth exceeding 100 m. in breadth. Its principal tributaries are the Ottawa which separates Upper and Lower Canada, and the Seguenay, which forms the outlet of Lake St. John. Its basin is estimated to contain 297,000 sq. m., of which 94,000 are covered with the lakes alone. The Orinoco, which notwithstanding the great length of its course occasioned by numerous detours to the W and E, is in its entire extent comprised within the limits of Colombia, rises in the mountainsystem of Parimé. The area of its basin is 252,000 sq. m. Its principal affluents are, on the right, the Ventuari and the Caura; on the left, occurs the bifurcation of the Cassiquiari, which by its junction with the Rio Negro, after a course of 120 m., connects the Orinoco with the Amazon; the other tributaries on the left are the Guaviare, the Meta, and the Apure. The Essequibo enters the Atlantic, between the Orinoco and the Amazon, by a mouth 14 m. wide. Its principal tributary is the Massaruni. The Amazon, sometimes called the Maranon, is formed by the confluence of the New Maranon or Tunguragua, with the Ucayali or Old Maranon. The former—commonly considered the head-stream of this great river—issues from Lake Llauricochain the Peruvian Andes. Balbi, however, is of opinion, with many other geographers, that the source of the Maranon ought rather to be identified with that of the Beni or Paro, which rises in the mountains of Sicasica in Bolivia, and after uniting with the Apurimac takes the name of the Ucayali, and directs its course to the northern confines of Peru, where it joins the New Maranon in the province of Maynas; after which, under the appellation of Solimoens, it proceeds in a general direction towards the E; at San Francisco de Tabatingo it enters the empire of Brazil; and, after traversing the immense province of Para from W to E, falls into the Atlantic; forming at its embouchure, with the confluence of the Para or Rio des Tocantins, the great island of Marajo. The principal affluents of the Amazon are, on the right, the Yavari and the Madeira, which in the length of their course rival the greatest rivers in the world; the Topayos, and the Xingu, all in the empire of Brazil; and on the left, besides the Tunguragua or New Maranon, the Napo, the Putumaya or Ica, the Caqueta or Yupura, and the Rio Negro with its tributaries, the Cassiquiari and the Rio Branco. The first of these affluents belongs to the Colombian territory, and the greater part of the second and the upper part of the Caqueta. The others water the Brazilian territory. The Tocantin, called the Para in the lower part of its course, is regarded by most geographers as an affluent of the Amazon with which it communicates by a natural canal called Tajipuru. It is formed by the junction of the Rio des Tocantins, or the Tocantin properly so called, with the Rio Grande, or Araguay, which is the principal branch. All the basin of this river belongs to the Brazilian territory. The San Francisco is one of the five great rivers of Brazil. It flows through the provinces of Minas Geraes, Pernambuco, Alagoas, and Sergipe. The appellation Rio de la Plata belongs, strictly speaking, to the united stream of the Parana and the Uraguay, on whose sea-like expanse are situated Monte Video, Buenos Ayres, and Maldonado. The Parana, of which the remote sources are to be found in the Brazilian province of Minas Geraes, is generally regarded as the principal branch. It traverses the SW part of Brazil, forms the separating line between that empire and the states of Paraguay and Rio de la Plata, and after traversing the southern part of the latter confederacy, unites its waters with that of the Uraguay. Its principal affluent, on the right, is the Paraguay, whose chief tributaries are the Pilcomayo, and the Rio Grande or Bermejo. The Tiete appears to be its principal affluent on the left. The Rio Negro or Cusu Lebu, is the most important river between the Plata and the strait of Magalhaens. It separates the pampas from the plain of Patagonia. That part of the Arctic sea called Hudson's bay receives two large rivers, viz., the Churchill or Missinipi, of which the true source has not yet been ascertained; and the Nelson, which is formed by the North and South Saskatchevan, whose united stream first flows into Lake Winnipeg, whence it issues under the name of the Nelson. The Severn also falls into this bay, but its origin and course are undetermined; and the Albany carries the waters of Lake St. Joseph to the bay. The gulf of Mexico receives the magnificent Mississippi, of which the entire course lies within the territory of the United States, and which occupies with its tributaries the whole of the southern portion of the great central basin which Balbi calls the plain of the Mississippi-Mackenzie. It is the greatest river of North America. Its principal affluents are the Missouri-which actually surpasses it both in length and volume of water,—the Arkansas and the Red river on the right; and the Ohio on the left.— The Rio del Norte or Rio Bravo, the greatest river of Mexico, also flows into this great gulf. The sea of the Antilles or Caribbean sea receives the Magdalena, which, rising in the central cordillera, traverses the Columbian provinces of Cundinamarca, Boyaca, and Magdalena, and enters the sea by several mouths. From the peculiar formation of its mountainchains, all the great rivers of America flow to the E, the N, or the S. The Pacific, notwithstanding the immense extent of its eastern coast, receives only a single river, the Oregon, or Colombia, the sources of which are in the Rocky mountains, and which traverses the country known as the Oregon territory. Its principal affluents are on the right, the Otchenankane, and, on the left, the Lewis, and the Multnomah: - The Rio Colorado, descending from the Sierra Verde, traverses New California, flows into the gulf of California, after receiving the Yaquesila and the Gila on the left. name of the Slave river, and which, after leaving Great Slave lake, is called the Mackenzie. Lakes.] The lakes of America constitute another of the most remarkable features of the Western world. The portion of the northern continent, comprised between the 42d and 67th northern parallels, and appropriately designated the Region of lakes, presents, in its immense inland seas, masses of fresh water unequalled in magnitude in any other section of the globe, besides a host of minor lakes and marshes too numerous for specification. In the rainy season, the inundations of the lakes form inter-communications with the great river-system of the northern continent; and thus temporarily connect the navigation of the northern with that of the southern waters of this great territory. The most remarkable for the magnitude of | ing to the season. expanse, and for the extent of the facilities which they afford to navigation, is the great series of lakes in the central section of the basin of the St. Lawrence. Michigan, . . . . . 1,000 —<br>Huran, . . . . . . 1,000 — <math>232 -</math> Ontario, It is computed that the lakes contain above 14,000 cubic miles of water, a quantity more than half of all the fresh water on the earth. The extent of country drained by the lakes from Niagara to the north-western angle of Superior, including also the area of the lakes themselves, is estimated at 335,515 sq. m. Belonging to the basin of the Nelson in British America are the Rainy lake, the Lake of the Woods, Lake Winnipeg, one of the greatest in America, 280 m. in length and from 15 m. to 80 m. in breadth, comprising an area of 9,000 sq. m., Lake Manitoba, Little Winnipeg, and many other minor lakes. Belonging to the basin of the Missinipi, or Churchill, are Lakes Buffalo, Cross, Black Bear, and Deer Lake which connects this basin with that of the Mackenzie. The basin of the Mackenzie comprises Lake Athabasca or the Lake of the mountains, the area of which is 3,000 sq. m. in extent; the Great Slave lake, with an area of 12,000 sq. m.; and the Great Bear lake, area 8,000 sq. m. Wollaston lake is common to the basin of the Missinipi and the Mackenzie. Numerous lakes have likewise been found to exist in the vast territories of New Wales, of Eastern Maine, the northern districts of Lower Canada, and other parts of British America; but they possess little geographical interest. The principal lakes in the basin of the Oregon or Colombia are the Timpanagos, in which the Multnomah has its source; and Lakes Otchenankane, Cutsamin or Eardbebs, and Flat-Bow. Belonging to the basin of the Tololotlan or Rio Grande, in the Mexican state of Xalisco, is Lake Chapala. In the valley of Tenochtitlan are Lakes Tezcuco, Xochimilco, Chalco, San-Christobal, and Zupango; and in New California, Lake Teguago. In Central America are Lake Nicaragua, one of the largest in America, 120 m. long and 40 in breadth, with its adjuncts Lakes Managua and Leon; and Lake Izaval, of which the Rio Golfo forms the outlet. Of the lakes of S America, one of the principal is Lake Titicaca, an isolated basin in Upper Peru, 300 m. in length and from 30 to 60 in breadth, covering a surface of 4,000 sq. m., and elevated several thousand feet above the level of the sea. The basin of the Rio Colorado or Mendoza, contains a number of marshy lakes, situated in the territory of the Rio de la Plata, of which Lake Guanacache is the most remarkable. In the basin of the Rio Negro are the Lagunes del Desaguadero, the Laguna Grande, and Lake Tehuel, sheets of water of great extent, but varying accord- Belonging to the vast basin of the Rio de la Plata is the great lake of Xarayes, which, during the rainy season, spreads its expanse hundreds of miles along the river. Of similar character is the lagune of Rogaguado, a vast but fluctuating lake, in the republic of Bolivia, which gives rise to several of the principal tributaries of the Amazon. In Venezuela are the small, but, from the richness of the surrounding agricultural districts, important lakes of Tacarigua and Valencia. To the N of Bogota, in the mountains of Zipaquira, celebrated for the mineral riches of the locality, is the small lake of Guatavita.—Lake Lauri or Lauricocha, in Peru, is of small dimensions, but is remarkable for the elevation of its situation, and for being the more generally accredited source of the Amazon.-The existence of Lake Parime, or the great White sea of the older maps, has been disproved by modern research. Climate.] The continent of America possesses of course every variety of climate, and a temperature varying with the distance from the equator; but it differs generally from the Eastern hemisphere by a greater predominance of cold. It is calculated that the heat is at least 10 degrees less upon an average on the American continent, than under the same parallels in Europe and Africa. Thus while Britain enjoys temperate seasons and mild air, Labrador, and the countries of the Esquimaux, though lying in the same parallel, are extremely cold; even the torrid zone in America knows none of those burning heats which are experienced in Asia and Africa. In the steppes of Caraccas—the hottest region of this continent—the heat seldom exceeds 98° in the shade. At Norfolk, on the 16th of February, 1798, snow fell in one night to the depth of 4 feet; and even at Charleston, in N lat. 32°, or the parallel of Marocco, the mercury has been known to fall 2° below 0° (32° Fahrenheit), according to Rochefoucault, and the ground freezes to the depth of 2 inches. In the severe winter of 1796, when the thermometer fell to 15° below 0°, the Alleghany, Monongahela, and Ohio were frozen over from the 28th Nov. to 30th Jan., -a space of 65 days; but this was considered without example. In one night, in 1808 and 1809, the Mississippi froze so as to bear horses and carriages at St. Genevieve next day. In Jan., 1805, the Mississippi was frozen to the depth of 22 inches at St. Louis in 38° 36' N lat. In 1796, the Mississippi and Ohio, with their tributary streams, were frozen to their junction. The Mississippi in 1802, 3, and 4, was passable on the ice with horses and carriages before the winter solstice. In three successive mornings the thermometer fell to 12° and 14° below zero; and in one morning of January it fell to 18° below zero. The above facts evince a degree of cold, even in the Western states, of which there is no experience in Europe in the same latitudes. It may, however, be affirmed that the climate of America is upon the whole not colder than under the same parallels in Eastern Asia. In America cultivation is extended to the parallel of 52° 50' N; in Asia it has reached 56° 30'; but in Europe it has been carried as high as 70° N. By the levelling of forests and the draining of marshes, the sun is let in upon the soil, and the climate rendered warmer, drier, and more salubrious; and it is affirmed that in North America the influence of these operations of human industry has already so far prevailed that the winters are much shorter, and the quantity of snow that falls considerably smaller. The St. Lawrence is nearly a month later in being shut up than when Canada was first settled; and the medium cold of winter has lost 8 degrees of its former severity in the neighbourhood of Quebec. Some parts too of the western territories of the United States, once esteemed exceedingly unhealthy, have, since the clearing away of the forests, become remarkable for salubrity. The great cause of the cold in North America has been attributed to the quantity of land stretching towards the North Pole, a proportion of which is involved in perpetual winter. The wind passing over this snow and iceclad tract brings, it is said, a severity of cold along with it which nothing can resist. In all parts of Northern America, the southern declivities of mountains and places sheltered from the N wind are warmer and more genial than other exposures. The western coast of North America, however, up to the parallel of 45°, approaches nearer in temperature to the climate of Europe than the eastern. In the valley of the Colombia it exceeds that of corresponding latitudes on the Atlantic coast by an amount equivalent to 5° or 6° of latitude. The whole of America N of the 55th parallel may be considered a frozen region. In Greenland and around Hudson's bay, brandy freezes during winter, which begins in August, and continues for nine months. In summer the heat is as great as in New England; but continues for too short a period to bring grain to maturity. Between 55° and 45° N the climate is still severe; the cold of winter is intense; and the snow, which begins to fall in November, remains till May. Here summer advances with such rapidity, that the season of spring is scarcely known. The temperate portions of North America may be considered as extending from 45° to 37° N; from the latter parallel to that of 40° S, the climate is hot, and the vegetation tropical; beyond S lat. 40° the climate again becomes cold; and the atmosphere, "where gale succeeds gale with rain, hail, and sleet, seems blacker than anywhere else." [Darwin.] Captain King notices a circumstance relative to the temperature of these regions which is very remarkable. While on the coast of Patagonia, though employed at his observatory the greater part of the night, when the thermometer was as low as the freezing-point, both within and outside of it, and, although not warmly clad, he felt no sort of inconvenience from the cold; in the winter-time also, the thermometer was occasionally at 24° without any inconvenience being felt. He attributes this to the peculiar stillness of the atmosphere on the coast, although at a short distance at sea, in the offing, the wind may be high. Another peculiarity in this climate is the extraordinary warmth of the sea near its surface, compared with the state of the atmosphere. In the month of June, Captain King observed a difference of 30° between the temperature of each; the consequence of which is that the sea is covered with a cloud of steam; and this may, in some measure, account for the prevalence of fogs. Another extraordinary circumstance relating to this climate is, that parrots and humming birds are numerous in the southern and western parts of Magalhaens strait: they were even observed on the wing during a snow-shower; and after a constant succession of rain, snow, and sleet, the latter have been seen sipping the sweets of the fuschia and other flowers, while the thermometer was at the freezingpoint. The character of excessive humidity has likewise been ascribed to the American continent; but it is universally acknowledged that, as cultivation has spread, the climate has grown sensibly milder, drier, and more salubrious. The climate of South America is generally superior to that of the northern part of the continent in all the districts north of the 50th parallel of southern latitude; but to the south of that line, the cold increases more rapidly than it does as we approach the pole in the Arctic regions. Among other causes which powerfully influence the temperature in South America and Central America must be reckoned the extraordinary elevation of the surface in many places. Thus the city of Mexico, which according to its latitude should be excessively hot, being elevated 7,000 ft. above sealevel, enjoys a climate of perpetual spring; and Quito, which lies under the equator, has a similar climate, though within sight of that city are regions, at an elevation of 15,000 or 16,000 feet, which are covered with never-changing masses of snow and ice; and at the distance of a few miles the inhabitants of Guyaquil, on a low and level margin of the sea, experience an intense and sickly degree of heat. South America is the great theatre at present of earthquakes, nor is North America free from them. "The three zones of temperature which originate in America," says Malte Brun, "from the enormous difference of level between the various regions, cannot by any means be compared with the zones which result from a difference of latitude. The agreeable, the salutary vicissitudes of the seasons are wanting in those regions that are here distinguished by the denominations of frigid, temperate, hot or torrid. In the frigid zone it is not the intensity but the continuance of the cold,-the absence of all vivid heat,-the constant humidity of a foggy atmosphere, -that arrest the growth of the great vegetable productions, and, in man, perpetuate those diseases that arise from checked perspiration. The hot zone of America does not experience excessive heat; but it is a continuance of the heat, together with exhalations from a marshy soil, and the miasmata of an immense mass of vegetable putrefaction, added to the effects of an extreme humidity, which produces fevers of a more or less destructive nature, and spreads through the whole animal and vegetable world the agitation of an exuberant but deranged vital principle. The temperate zone, by possessing only a moderate and constant warmth like that of a hot-house, excludes from its limits the animals and vegetables which delight in the extremes of heat and cold, and produces its own peculiar plants, which can neither grow above its limits, nor descend below them. Its temperature, which does not brace the constitution of its constant inhabitants, acts like spring on the diseases of the hot regions, and like summer on those of the frozen regions: accordingly, a mere journey from the Andes to the level of the sea, or vice versa, proves an important medical agent, which is sufficient to produce the most astonishing changes in the human body. But, living constantly in either one or the other of these zones must enervate both the mind and the body by its monotonous tranquillity. Summer, Spring, and Winter are here seated on three distinct thrones, which they seldom quit, and are constantly surrounded by the attributes of their Botany.] The vegetable kingdom of America throughout all power." its regions, but especially in those of the equatorial zone, exceeds in the variety, luxuriance, and multiplicity of its productions the botanical riches of any of the corresponding climates of the Old world. Its indigenous flora already numbers in its classifications upwards of 15,000 phanerogami. The most northerly latitude in which vegetation has been discovered is Melville's island, in 74° 30' N lat. In this desolate region such vegetable species as grasses, saxifrages, mosses, and lichens find existence, and the Protococus nivalis, which exists in even higher latitudes, tinges the snows with its crimson flowerets; but the only plant of woody structure it is capable of producing, is the Arctic willow, which here attains only six inches in height. The vegetation of Greenland, and of the coasts of Baffin and Hudson's bay, bears a close affinity to that of the High Alps or of Lapland, consisting of stunted willows (Salix herbacea, retusa, and reticulata), birches (Betula alba, and nana), poplars (Populus trepida), pines, a few species of herbaceous plants, remarkable for the large size of their flowers and the rapidity of their development, and different species of cryptogami, which are exceedingly abundant, covering to a great extent the soil of these polar regions. Proceeding southwards, we meet with vast forests of spruce firs (Abies alba and nigra), beneath which the rein-deer moss, and other lichens, overspread the soil, and various berry-bearing shrubs and papilionaceous plants. Next follow the majestic poplars of Canada (Populus Hudsonica), &c., birches (Betula papyracea and populifolia), various oaks, ashes, butternuts (Juglans cinerea), and hickories (Carya alba and amara). On the southern frontier of the British possessions the sugar-maple and azaleas abound, many kinds of asters stud the woods and meadows with their star-like flowers, and wheat, maize, and even tobacco, form common field crops, marking the transition to the flora of the United States. The American elm is properly a Canadian tree, as it is in the N that this tree—the most magnificent of the temperate zone—attains its finest proportions. The botanical region of the United States, including the whole central district of N America, from about 50° to 25° N lat., consists of the vast and originally uninterrupted forest-tract, extending from Hudson's bay to the Mexican gulf, and westwards—but confined to the banks of the rivers— far beyond the Mississippi. Some prairies or unwooded tracts occur in Illinois, Indiana, in the N of Ohio, and in the southern districts of Mississippi and Alabama. Of the 140 species of trees which are found in this forest, more than 80 attain a height of 60 feet and upwards. The most characteristic are the hickories, the tupelos, the lyriodendron or tulip-tree, the taxodium or American cypress, the locust and coffee trees, and the negundo. It likewise presents numerous species of the oak, ash, and pine, and possesses several of the magnolia tribe, one of the Gordonia, the sycamore or buttonwood, the liquidambar, and the tree-andromeda, two species of the walnut, three of the tilia, the red-bay, hackberry, &c. The shrubs and herbaceous plants which form the undergrowth of this forest belong generally to the classes which require more or less protection from the sun. In the prairie-regions where the grasses usurp the domain of trees and shrubs, the northern districts present a strong analogy to the Tartarian steppes, not only in their physical aspect and numerous salines, but in the gay profusion of their floral vegetation. In their SW parts vegetation is very thinly diffused; and towards the Rocky mountains it is so scanty that the name of desert has been given to an extensive tract; but there is no district altogether destitute of streams, or where cactuses and yuccas may not occasionally be met with, or even some cucurbitaceous plants and grape-vines spreading over the sands. The western district, which appears to be less extensive than the eastern, contains fewer but not less gigantic species. Spruces prevail in the northern; pines, maples, oaks and poplars in the central district; The most characteristic features of the North American flora and pines in the southern region. are exhibited in the United States. Here the forests consist of pines and larches unknown in the Old world, of many kinds of oaks, of locust-trees (Robinia pseudacacia), black walnuts of enormous size, hickories, and ashes, among which the noble tulip-tree (Lyriodendron-tulipifera) rears its towering head. In the swamps grow the deciduous cypress (Taxodium distichum), the white cedar (Cupresses thyoides), two species of fir (Pinus serotina and Abies pendula), the rhododendron or rose bay, the glaucous kalmia, andromedas, sarracenias, and the glaucous magnolia. The sides of the mountains are covered with the arbor vitæ (Thuya occidentalis), with magnolias, and hemlock-spruces (Abies Canadensis), intermingled with the arborescent azaleas, the sorrel-tree (Andromeda arborea), and the beautiful mountain laurel (Kalmia latifolia). The undergrowth of the woods and plains contains endless varieties of the aster, several species of azalea and asclepias, the dwarf pyrus, and the exclusively American genera of liatris, phlox, &c. Tobacco, maize, and wheat are the staple articles of cultivation. Of the aquatic plants,-many of which are remarkable for their beauty,-the principal are the hydropeltis, the orontium, various singular sagittarias or arrowheads, the white and the yellow water-lily, the Vallisneria Americana, used as food by the canvass-back ducks, the pickerel-weed (Pontederia cordata), &c. The gramineous species contain several grasses of peculiar forms, numerous rushes, the large and beautiful wild-rice (Zizania aquatica), some carices, &c. Of the indigenous ferns-which are very numerous-the United States possess none in common with the Old world. In the more southern districts, from 35° to 25° N lat., the vegetation becomes more varied and characteristic. In addition to the greater number of the above-mentioned plants it includes many belonging to warmer temperatures. Amongst the climbing plants, which are very numerous, are clematis, vines, passifloras, tillandsias, &c. Among the herbaceous and smaller plants are several lupines, sarracenias, gentians, the fly-trap (Dionæa muscipula), common in swampy situations but like the Cabomba aquatica indigenous likewise in tropical climates, sundews, asarums, amarylli, the superb yuccas, gerardias, parietarias, &c. Aquatic plants also abound, such as the magnificent nelumbium, the nuphar, lobelias, &c.; and the cane, -a gigantic grass, - (Miegia macrosperma), occupies extensive tracts. To the W of the Rocky mountains we are presented with an entirely new botanical region distinguished by characteristic productions, but possessing some species in common with the eastern districts of the American continent. A single pœonia, the only species of that plant indigenous to America, belongs to this region. The vegetation of the NW coast bears considerable affinity to that of the United States and of the opposite shores of Asia. The Clarckia pulchella, Coreopsis tinctoria, Escholtzia California, and several species of cenotheras and lupines are natives of that region. In the southern states, the climate of which exceeds in heat and humidity that of any other corresponding latitude, a botanical region is presented in which the productions of Mexico are commingled with those of the north. Here, in addition to the principal productions of Virginia and Kentucky, cotton, indigo, and the sugar-cane are objects of cultivation, and the plane and deciduous cypress acquire gigantic dimensions. A solitary epidendron inhabits the branches of the magnolia near Savanna; and in the same locality is found the Pinckneya, a plant allied to the Peruvian bark. The parasitical gigantic longmoss (Tillandsia usneoides) is exceedingly abundant in all the forests of the Carolinas, Florida, Alabama, &c. Southern Mexico exhibits extreme diversity in its vegetable productions; while its coasts, as well as the shores of the Antilles, present those of tropical regions, the botany of its higher elevations bears considerable affinity to that of temperate latitudes, but exhibits some productions closely related to equatorial species. The principal productions belonging to the warm regions of Mexico are palms, bananas (Musa paradisiaca), plantains (Musa sapientum), yams, coffee, indigo, sugar-cane, maize, the cocoa-tree (Theobroma cacoa), the pine-apple, which grows wild in the woods, the American-aloe and various cactuses which abound in localities where nothing else can find existence. The low forests of Honduras produce immense quantities of mahogany (Swietenia Mahogani), logwood trees, tamarinds, lignum vitæ (Guiacum officinale), and vanilla (Epidendron vanilla). The jalap (Convolvulus jalapa), abounds near the city from which it derives its name. Of the numerous productions of the more elevated or temperate regions of Mexico, the principal are oaks (Quercus xalapensis, obtusata, glaucescens and laurina), Taxus montana, Erythroxylum Mexicanum, and Dahlia-cobæa scandens. The higher elevations exhibit many species of plants belonging to European genera, such as valerians, roses, violets, salvias, &c. The Cheirostemon platanoides, a tree remarkable for the beauty and singularity of its organization, forms immense forests in the northern vicinity of Toluca. The caryophylaceous and rhodoraceous tribes common to northern climates here form the vegetation of altitudes verging on the regions of perpetual snow. The indigenous vegetation of the lower districts of the equatorial regions of America is characterised by extreme luxuriance and diversity. In the very general enumeration to which we are necessarily restricted in this article, we may notice as among the most important, from their local and commercial utility, the palms,—of which 80 species distinguished for their size, beauty and productiveness,-are extensively diffused over all the plains and minor acclivities of Columbia, Guayana and Brazil. The Ceroxylon andicola has been met with on the slopes of considerably elevated mountains, such as those of Quindiu in Peru. The araucaria of Chili and Brazil, the cocoa, the rocon (Bixa Orellana), the dye-wood of Campeachy (Hamatoxylon Campeachanium), and the banana may also be specified. The Brazilian forests contain no fewer than 259 species of wood useful for carpentry or dyeing; and 14 species of the cinchona or Peruvian bark are produced in various districts; guaiacum, or lignum vitæ is abundant, and the caoutchouc or elastic gum is found in Guayana, Brazil and Buenos Ayres. The cow-tree is confined to Venezuela. The species of cyatheæ (C. arborea, speciosa), &c., and the feterides, aspides, doradilles (Asplenium arboreum), constitute some of the most striking characteristics of the flora of the Antilles, New Andalusia, Caripi, New Grenada, environs of Guaduas and Icononzo, of the valleys of Peru between Loxo and the Amazon, of Mexico and of the country around Xalappa. The arborescent ferms and cacti-of which the Cactus coccinillifer is one of the most remarkable—likewise present here a highly indigenous character. The most important products of cultivation are the coffee-tree, sugar-cane, cotton-plant, bread-fruit-tree and indigo plant, &c. Above the level of 3,100 ft. all these plants are only to be found in advantageous situations; but the sugar-cane can be successfully cultivated at the height of 7,500 ft. Wheat grows at the elevation of 3,000 ft., but attains its greatest vigour at about 4,500 ft. Barley is raised at the height of 4,800 ft. and upwards to 6,000 ft. The potato (Solanum tuberosum), and maize (Mais zea), are natives of this continent, but to what particular locality they originally belonged is not agreed upon by botanists. The banana is occasionally met with at an altitude of 8,000 ft.; but its fruit does not ripen much above the height of 5,000 ft. The region comprehended between 4,920 and 5,160 ft. abounds also with the cocoa or Erythroxylum Peruvianum, a few leaves of which mixed with quicklime support the Peruvian Indian in his longest journeys over the cordillera. The cultivation of the Chenopodium quinoa and of the European gramini is most successful in the extensive plateaus of the Andes at an elevation of 6,000 to 9,000 ft.; above the height of 9,600 ft. the wheat crops are often destroyed by frost. Indian corn does not grow at a higher elevation than 7,200 ft. About 1,000 ft. higher than this the potato presents itself; but it ceases to grow at 12,600 ft. At about 10,200 ft. barley disappears, and rye can with difficulty be raised. Above the altitude of 11,040 ft. cultivation altogether ceases. "In the equinoctial countries of A. vegetation displays itself to the observer as on the gradually rising steps of an immense amphitheatre, the base of which sinks below the ocean, while the summit reaches to the snow-line of the Andes, 15,000 ft. above sea-level: showing that in A. vegetation attains a height of 1,600 or 1,800 yards beyond the point where it ceases on the Pyrenees and Alps,-a difference which M. Ramond thinks is dependent upon the breadth or thickness of a mountain-chain as well as upon latitude." [Mirbel.] The botanical productions of the more southerly regions of the continent bear a close affinity to those of Europe. The plains of the Rio de la Plata and of the Uraguay are much infested by the Cinara cardunculus, a species of thistle common to France. Of the vegetation of Patagonia and the adjacent archipelagoes, little is yet known, but in its general character it appears to present considerable analogy to that of northern latitudes; although exhibiting - especially in its cryptogami - very different con- 1,200 ft.; and the line of perpetual snow descends as low as 3,000 ft. The coasts generally present a very similar botanical aspect; but it is to be inferred that the high lands of the interior of Tierra del Fuego possess a very different vegetation from that of the level of the Falkland isles, with the flora of which scientific research is comparatively well-acquainted. The largest vegetable production of these islands, which attains no greater height than 6 ft., belongs to the genus veronica. Several species of gramineæ, and of ferns, mosses and lichens constitute the predominant vegetation. The Bolax glebaria exists in Tierra del Fuego. America does not contain a single species of heath; but it is the native soil of the fuchsia, the Calathea mustisia, and of all the bromeliaceæ. Zoology.] The animal kingdom of America presents numerous genera altogether distinct from the aborigines of any other portion of the globe. The Arctic shores possess many animals in common with the adjacent steppes of Siberia; and the zoology of Northern America generally exhibits a close analogy to that of corresponding latitudes on the eastern continents; but in its intertropical regions, which are characterised by all the variety and abundance that distinguish the equatorial zone of the Old world, we scarcely identify a single species as common to the two continents.—Of the quadrumana, America possesses, in the monkey-tribe, numerous species altogether distinct from those of Africa or Asia. Of these the most remarkable are the long-armed ateles, which lives in the torrid regions of Brazil, Guayana, and New Spain, the prehensile-tailed lagotriches, the marauding sapajous, the gentle sagoins, the nocturnal douroucoli, the bearded sakis, the fantastic and gaudy oristiti, and the rosalia or marikina.—Of the cheiroptera, America does not possess a single species of the fructivora family, which are exclusively Asiatic; but of the bat tribe, it has numerous species of the vampire or spectre-bat.—Of the plantigrade carnivora, several species haunt the old forests of the Rocky mountains, and the plains of the Columbia and Coppermine rivers. Of these the most formidable are the brown, grey, black, and white bears, and especially the Ursus horribilis. The Ursus ornatus is confined to the Cordilleras. The water-rat infests all parts of America. The coati and the prehensile-tailed kinkajou belong to Brazil. The cold regions of Labrador and of the Esquimaux coast are frequented by the sable, the wolverine, the badger, the bison, and various species of the martin tribe. Otters are taken in considerable numbers on the NW coast; and in the northern districts of America foxes and dogs are extremely numerous. The most valuable species of the latter are those of the Esquimaux territories and Newfoundland. The black wolf frequents the arctic regions, and the red wolf, or agurara-guazu, is common in Paraguay, Mexico, and the prairies of the Missouri.—Of the feline tribe America possesses several of the larger species, of which the most formidable are the cuguacuarana or Peruvian lion, the black cuguacuarana of Cayenne, the jaguar, the yaguarondi of Chili, and the ocelot. Several species of the lynx are common in Canada; and the eyra, the margay, the chati, and the collocolla, abound in the various regions of America. - Of marsupalia, America possesses several peculiar aboriginal species. Of these the principal are the opossum of Canada, and the Cheironecles palmata, or yapuk of Guayana.—Among numerous species of rodentia, the beaver is now almost confined to Canada and the north-west territories; squirrels are numerous in all quarters; the musk-beaver or musquash is found in Canada; the singular viscaches and the beautiful chinchilla are likewise aborigines of the New world. The Brazilian porcupine or coando, and the orico, abound in Canada, and are common to various districts of America. Rabbits, hares, and tapeti, abound in all parts of the continent; the pacas, agoutis, cobayes or Guinea pigs, are confined to Paraguay, Brazil, and Guayana. The armadillo, the ant-eaters, and sloths, are peculiar to America; and the grotesque chlamyphorus is confined to the skirts of the Andes; two species of the tapir, of which one frequents the marsh lands of the torrid zone, and the other the declivities of the Cordilleras, the maipuri and the panchaque, represent the gigantic solipeda; the peccaris represent the wild boar; and the llamas, vigognas, and alpacas, the cameloida of the Old world. The reindeer of the Arctic regions extends its haunts to the gulf of the St. Lawrence; the slender wapiti and the Virginian stag are the aboriginal representatives of the gazelle and antelope families. The bison or buffalo, and the musk ox, are the largest quadrupeds possessed by the United States. The Rocky mountain goat, (C. Americana,) remarkable for its long silky hair, is confined to the regions of that mountain range. The winged tribes of America equal the preceding classes in variety, and surpass those of any other portion of the globe in the beauty of their colouring and conformation. The ostrich of Africa, and the cassowary of Malasia and Australia, are represented in this continent by the nandu, which abounds in the plains of Patagonia. The sariama, which inhabits the savannahs of Guayana, bears a close affinity to the messenger-bird of the Cape of Good Hope. Of the numerous accipitres, which are found in all its districts, the principal species are the condor, (Vultur gryphus,) which lives in the solitudes of the Andes, and the king vulture (Sarcoramphus papa). Of the falcon tribe are the iribius, the rancancas, the chima-chima, the laughing macaguas, the brilliant elanus and nauclerus, and numerous species of hawks, buzzards, and choughs. The beautiful toucans, and the gelden couroucous or tregons, inhabit the equatorial region of the continent; the sasa is confined to the marshy districts of Guayana. Of the parrot tribe America possesses numerous species; and its formations. Trees reach up the mountain-sides to an elevation of | hottest regions present the emerald jacamars and jacamerops, with the kindred species of woodpeckers and alcyons, manakins, the beautiful-crested rupicoles, the brilliant colibris, and the gemlike tribes of trochilidæ. The peacock and the pheasant have no representatives in this continent, but their place is supplied by the turkey, the courasu, and the guan. The tetrao and perdrix families are numerous in the north. The coasts swarm with lapwings, plovers, spoonbills, flamingos, herons, and pelicans. The rivers of Mexico, Louisiana, Florida, and the southern states of the Anglo-Americans, are infested by various tribes of alligators. Six species of the rattlesnake belong to the low coast lands of America and of the Antilles, between 45° N lat. and the Rio de la Plata. Several species of the deadly trigonocephales inhabit the forests of Brazil and some of the islands of the Lesser Antilles.—Of the finny tribes, many species common to the Old world abound in the seas and rivers of America. In the numerous catalogue which their varieties present, those of most commercial utility are the whales, dolphins, cachelots, which frequent the northern seas, the seals of Newfoundland and Cape Horn; the shoals of cod which inhabit the great banks of Newfoundland. -The terrestrial and fresh-water mollusca, the insect and zoophyte classes, present in their respective orders the same variety of species which characterises the higher grades of animal existence. Those of the southern hemisphere are highly distinctive. Of insect tribes, the mosquitoes are the scourge not of the tropical regions only, but even in the far north; while the chigoe is scarcely less dreaded. Centipedes, white ants, and termita, also abound. Minerals.] This continent, particularly South America and Mexico, abounds in gold and silver. It also produces copper, quicksilver, iron, antimony, sulphur, nitre, lead, loadstone, marble, the diamond, pearl, emerald, and amethyst. Rock and springsalt is of frequent occurrence throughout America. Ethnography.] America presents in its ethnography, as compared with the other great continents, the anomaly of the smallest population in proportion to extent and surface, with the greatest diversity of tribes. The entire number of its inhabitants is estimated at 44,000,000,—a number not exceeding that of the united nations of France and Belgium,—and presenting the relatively scanty population of 3.5 to every square mile of its superficial area; while within the same space, Oceania exhibits the proportion of 6.5, Africa 7, Asia 32, and Europe 82. The aborigines of America, amounting probably to 10,000,000, are lingually distributed into a multiplicity of families, represented by 438 languages and upwards of 2,000 dialects, identified by many striking analogies in all their varieties with a common origin, and yet characterised by certain physical and moral peculiarities which distinguish them from the inhabitants of any other section of the world. The name Indians is common to the aborigines of the New continent. We can give no opinion respecting their origin. The only hypothesis on this subject, founded on any better evidence than conjecture, is that America was peopled by the way of Behring's strait. It is certain that an easy communication has existed between the two continents at this point for several centuries. Arguing from this fact merely, it is as easy to prove that the Old world received its inhabitants from the New, as the contrary. But since it is not only possible, but unquestionable, that the whole human race are varieties of the same species, and since the Word of God assures us that they are all descended from one male and female, it remains for us to show in what manner the descendants of this stock may have reached America, and whether the theory we may propose can be supported by arguments drawn from the condition of the New world. "A reference to a map of the globe will show us that immediately within the Arctic circle, the eastern extremity of the Old continent is separated from the New by a strait which is but 49 m. across; and this is solidly frozen over during the severities of winter. Kamtschatka, the extremity of Asia, situated between the 40th and 50th degree of N. lat., is peopled by natives who are thoroughly accustomed to endure all the rigours of this climate, and is provided with many animals equally capable of existing through all its inclemencies. Under such circumstances we can see no difficulty in concluding that from the eastern extremity of Asia, both men and animals have passed to America, and subsequently been multiplied over the whole continent. In regard to man, it is not necessary to insist that he passed to the American shore during winter, since the distance is not too great for us to believe that even the rudest navigators, when driven by stress of weather from their own coastas often happens to the Esquimaux-could with little difficulty reach this continent, where they would be compelled to remain by necessity, or induced by a disposition either to extend their acquaintance with a strange land, or to seek for a more agreeable place of abode. Indeed the Aleutian islands, which are very numerous, and form an almost perfectly continuous chain, beginning with Behring's island, and extending from opposite to Kamtschatka, in about the 55th degree of N lat., to Alaska, under the same parallel in America, may have afforded a much easier and more certain approach, and that without appearing at all extraordinary to the voyagers themselves, who might pass from one isle to another without having any idea of the main to which they were approaching. These islands are in the same parallels of latitude as the greater part of Labrador, Hudson's bay, &c., where even Europeans are able to endure the climate during the severest seasons. There is, in fact, the most irrefragable testimony to prove that reindeer cross over in vast herds on the ice, subsisting on the moss found in these islands during their passage. But in strictness of reasoning we have nothing to make it absolutely necessary that we should refer the peopling of America to a period subsequent to the separation of the Old and the New continents by Behring's strait. There is neither extravagance nor impropriety in the opinion that the two continents were originally one; and being continuous, the only difficulty is removed that could be urged against the approach of population from the extremity of Asia. In addition to all the reasons that can be urged in support of this doctrine, we maintain it should not be forgotten that there are strong evidences from astronomical and geological observations, that the axis and poles of our globe are not now precisely where they originally stood. It is therefore unfair to decide against the probability of peopling America from the extremity of Asia, reasoning from the existing climate of the countries adjacent to East cape, or cape Prince of Wales, the two nearest points of Asia and America. The greatest difficulty thrown in the way of this opinion, was thought to be the striking difference between the Esquimaux and the common Indian, seeming to prove that they were derived from different races or kinds. We are informed in Crantz' 'History of Greenland,' that the Moravian missionaries who visited the countries inhabited by the Esquimaux, were much surprised to find that they were in all respects similar to the Greenlanders, and made use of the same language; showing that the Esquimaux had sprung from the same race, and had gradually reached their present residence from the extreme northern parts of Europe. This fact—now rendered undeniable by more recent researches—entirely invalidates the conclusion that the Esquimaux were derived from another species. The resemblance existing between these people and the Siberians, Kamtschadales, Tunguse, &c., is manifest; notwithstanding they differ in many respects from other inhabitants of the New world, they are undeniably descended from the same parent-stock, coming from different parts of the globe. The copper-coloured natives of America, who are the most numerous of the aborigines, approach more closely to the Asiatic Tartars in colour and stature, because they are descendants of that race arriving in America from the extremity of Asia." [Goodman.] With the exception, perhaps, of the Esquimaux, all the Indians have the same physical characteristics. The bronze or copper colour,—the straight, coarse, black hair,—the hazel eyes,—the high cheek-bones and erect form, -are common to them all. There is, indeed, some difference in the stature of different tribes. The Osages are very tall, and the Shoshonees are below the middle stature. Each race, and indeed each tribe, has its peculiar physiognomy. To a European or Anglo-American, all Indians look alike; but one accustomed to them can distinguish their tribes with almost unerring certainty. Thus a Dahcotah is as readily distinguished from a Chippeway or a Winnebago by his features as his dress. Yet the difference is not so great as to induce a belief that all the tribes are not descended from the same stock. The Esquimaux of Greenland and the eastern part of the continent differ from the red Indians in complexion and stature, and in the position of the eyes, which are set obliquely in their orbits. As we go westward, along the northern shore of America, we find the Esquimaux as tall as other races of men; and after passing the mouth of the Mackenzie river, they blend with the Indians in every particular, so that it is hard to say where the Esquimaux become Indians, or where the Indians become Esquimaux. As low on the coast of the Pacific as Nootka sound, the natives have some characteristics of the Esquimaux race. Whether these people be of the same stock as the Indians or not, it is almost certain they have had a common origin with the savages of the northern shores of the old continent. Perhaps the diminutive stature of the Eastern Esquimaux is owing to their mode of living, which continually exposes them to every hardship and privation, There is another point of difference between this people and other Indians: from Cape Farewell to Behring's strait, the Esquimaux speak one language, and derive almost their whole subsistence from the sea; whereas the red Indians never resort to fishing when they can do otherwise; and speak a great variety of dialects, even when the language of the several tribes is radically the same. The Indians in the southern part of North America have been subject to the Spaniards, and are now dependent on the republics of Mexico and Guatemala, if we except some tribes, such as the Apaches, the Nabajoas, and the Mosquitos. The independent tribes of the north of Mexico resemble those of the United States in manners and customs. Living by the chase and plunder, and provided with fleet horses, they harass the frontiers and hunters. On the coasts of Yucatan, they live by hunting, fishing, and the trade in dye-wood. The Mexican Indians are grave, melancholy, and silent; their music and dances display the same character. The Indians of South America do not differ materially, in their physical characteristics, from those of the northern half of the continent; and, except those of Peru and Chili, are without civilization. In the extensive regions formerly belonging to Spain, they may be divided into two classes,—the independent Indians, or Indios bravos,— and those who have been reduced to submission. The former are entirely strangers to agriculture, and support themselves by the chase and fishing. The natives of Peru, descendants of the ancient inhabitants of the empire of the Incas, as well as those of Colombia, have been emancipated since those countries delivered themselves from the Spanish yoke, for their services during the war of the colonies against the mother-country. They are, in general, well made and healthy. The Indians of Chili are mostly independent. Their features are regular, and their complexion is not very dark. Their principal wealth consists in herds of oxen, horses, and guanacos. At the southern extremity of South America are the Patagonians, who have large nervous frames, a dark complexion, a flat nose, high cheek-bones, and a large mouth. The most remarkable of the aboriginal tribes of America are the following: The Pecherais or Yucanacus, who inhabit the Terra del Fuegan archipelago, and some districts of the adjacent coast of Patagonia; and who form one of the most degraded and personally repulsive branches of the human family. They locate themselves on the sea-shore, and feed on the mollusca. Their number is estimated at 2,000. The Tehuelhets, a numerous race, subdivided into a variety of tribes, and some of them of great stature, are scattered over the vast region of Patagonia, from the straits of Magellan to the Rio Camarones. The Chilian family comprises several tribes who live in the upper vallies of the N and E of Chili, and who have spread from Southern Chili into Patagonia. The most numerous and important of these tribes are, 1st. The Ancas or Moloushes, called by the Spaniards Araucanians, who inhabit the districts W of the Andes, extending along the shores of the Pacific, from the Biobio southward to the Valdivia. They form the powerful Araucanian confederation. 2d. The Vuta-Huilliche tribes, located to the S of the Araucanians, and along the W coast of Patagonia, of whom the principal are the Cunchi, the Chonos, the Poy-yus, and the Key-yus. The mountaineers of these tribes are of lofty stature, and are good and enterprising horsemen. The Puelches consist of several tribes, some of whom are called Pampas by the Spaniards. They formerly inhabited the southern districts of Buenos Ayres, between the Rio-Colorado and Rio- Negro. They were the most warlike of the S American nations; and for many years maintained an incessant struggle with the surrounding republics. Only a small remnant of them now exists in the fastnesses of the Andes. To the Alocoby-Abypon family, a tall and athletic race, belong 1st. The Mocobies, a warlike tribe who inhabit Chaco; and 2d. The Abipones, whose number has been greatly reduced by frequent conflicts with the Mocobies. The Peruvian or Quichua family comprises, 1st. The Peruvians, who form the large proportion of the population of Peru, of Bolivia, and of the southern departments of Columbia; and who at the period at which they were first visited by the Spaniards were —as their hieroglyphic painting and architectural antiquities attest—one of the most advanced of the S American nations in the arts of civilised life. 2d. The Aymaras or Aymares, a family consisting of several tribes in the diocese of Paz, and in Chiquisaca in Bolivia. The Chiquitos are a numerous people, scattered over the vast region which bears their name, belonging to the republic of Bolivia. They profess Christianity. The Carapuchos, a reputed anthropophagic tribe, inhabit the banks of the Pachitea in Peru. The Guarani family consists of four principal tribes, subdivided into numerous hordes, and scattered over Brazil and the greater part of the southern district of Spanish America. The most important of these tribes are: 1st. The Guaranis properly so called, who inhabit the banks of the Parana, the Uraguay and the Ibicuy. Of their powerful association which was constituted under the direction of the Jesuits towards the middle of the 18th century, the only remains are the seven missions in the Brazilian province of San Pedro, and the mission district on the Parana in Paraguay. 2d. The Brazilians, formerly comprising a variety of tribes scattered over the entire territory of Brazil, but now reduced to a very small number. 3d. The Omoguas, who inhabit the banks of the Amazon, and are celebrated for their skill in navigating the mighty streams of the S American continent. The Botecudos or Engerecmoung, formerly called Aymores or Amboures, are a reputed anthropophagic tribe, who inhabit the banks of the Rio-Doce and Rio Belmonte; and are scattered over the entire district of the coast of Brazil, between the former river and the Rio Prado. The Mundrucus, a fierce and warlike people, the most numerous and powerful in the province of Para, inhabit the district between the Xingu and the Tapayos. The greater number of the Mundrucus tribes are now in alliance with the Portuguese. The Payagua-Guaycuru family is composed of five principal branches, of which the two most important are: 1st. The Payagua, who inhabit the vicinity of Assompcion in Paraguay, formerly a numerous and enterprising tribe of lofty stature and skilful in navigation. 2d. The Guaycurus, called also Cavallieros, from their practice of using horses in their military expeditions, an athletic race who inhabit the banks of the Upper Paraguay, living on the produce of the chase and of fishing, and maintaining numerous herds of cattle. The Mexican family consist of several branches, of whom the principal are: 1st. The Mexicans or Aztecks, who occupy nearly the entire extent of the territory stretching from the 37th parallel to the lake of Nicaragua. The numerous monuments of their early attainments in art and science, in their pyramids, canals, and roads, their idiographic writing, maps, division of time, &c., and their civil, military, and religious institutions, assign to this people the highest rank in the intellectual scale of the numerous races which belong to the New world. 2d. The Moqui, a peaceable, agricultural, and somewhat civilized race, inhabit the northern bank of the Yaquesila. The Apaches, a numerous and warlike people, divided into several tribes, are scattered over the entire extent of the province of San-Luis-de-Potosi, as far as the northern extremity of the gulf of California. They are the inveterate enemies of the Jetans and Spaniards, and are generally of nomadic habits. Their warriors are commonly mounted, and are armed with long lances. The Panis-Arrapahoes family consists of several tribes, of whom the principal are: 1st. The Panis or Pawnees, a numerous and warlike people, who live in three large villages on the banks of the Wolf river, one of the tributaries of the Platte, and maintain constant war with the Sioux, Osages, Conzas, Corneilles, &c. With the exception of the Skeye, or Wolf Pawnees, one of the most powerful of its tribes, who have relinquished the practice, the Pawnees are said to be addicted to the use of human sacrifices in their worship. 2d. The Arrapahoes or Arrapahays, a numerous and warlike race, who frequent the banks of the Platte, between the Pawnees and the Canenawisch. They are of nomadic habits, and are excellent horsemen, and form a confederation sufficiently formidable to occasion serious harassment to the eastern and northern frontiers of the Mexican states. 3d. The Jetans, Teutans or Tetans, a numerous and powerful nomadic race, named by the Spaniards Camanches or Cumanches, and by the Pawnees and Osages, Paducas, who roam over the vast district comprised within the sources of the Missouri, the Upper Arkansas, the Trinidad, the Rio Brassos de Dios, the Rio Colorado and the Rio Bravo del Norte and the Sierra Madre and Sierra dos Mimbres. The Tushepaws, who inhabit the district in the vicinity of the sources of the Missouri and Columbia and which extends to the S of the latter river. 2d. The Multnomah, of whom the principal branch inhabits the island of Wappatoo, formed by the confluence of the Multnomah with the Columbia. 3d. The Chahala, whose principal branch inhabits the right bank of the Columbia river above the junction of the Canoe. 4th. The Snake Indians, called also Alliatan and Shoshonees, and by the English, Flat-heads - from the practice to which they are addicted of flattening their children's heads-inhabit the banks of the southern tributaries of the Columbia, especially of the Lewis and Multnomah. They are generally of mild dispositions, live in large well-built cabins, and subsist almost exclusively on roots and fish. 4th. The Shoshonees. 5th. The Chopunish. 6th. The Sokulks. 7th. The Esheluts. 8th. The Enishurs; and 9th. The Chilluckittequaws, all of whom are adept equestrians. The Sioux-Osage family comprises a number of independent tribes, of whom the principal are: 1st. The Sioux or Dacotas called also Ochenti-Shakong, Narkotah, and Nadowessies, the most numerous and most powerful of the independent aboriginal races of N America. They are divided into numerous tribes, of whom the most important are: The Dacotas, properly so called, who inhabit the wide district which extends along the middle Missouri, and stretches from St. Peter's river up the Mississippi, and north to Lake Winnipeg from the 42d to the 49th parallel. They are subdivided into numerous tribes, and form a powerful confederation. 2d. The Assiniboins or Assinepotuc, called also Stone-Sioux, and by the Dacotas-with whom they have maintained a sanguinary feud since the commencement of the 17th century - Hota, live in alliance with the Chippeways to the N of the Dacotas, occupying the extensive region which stretches from Lake Winnipeg to the Saskatchevan river and frequently extending their incursions against their enemies the Blackfeet as far as the mountain-ranges of the Columbian - Missouri. The Sioux form a confederation of independent tribes uniting in general council on occasions of common interest, when they are represented by deputies—each of whom attaches to the hieroglyphic inscription in which are wont to be commemorated the transactions of the assembly - the tabellionat or blazonry of his tribe. The Sioux commence the year at the vernal equinox. They have no hebdomadal division of time, and reckon it by sleeps or nights. 3d. The Omawha or Maha, who are subdivided into several tribes and whose principal location is a large village on the Elk-Horn, one of the northern tributaries of the Platte. They distinguish by names the pole-star, Venus, and several of the principal constellations. 4th. The Mandanes, a small tribe who inhabit two villages on the Upper Missouri and live on amicable terms with the whites. They are remarkable for the singularity of their religious tenets, and for the fairness of complexion by which some of their number are distinguished. 5th. The Wawsash, commonly called Osages, a brave and warlike race divided into three principal tribes, who live in villages and who have made considerable progress in civilization. They, like the Maha, have made some astronomical observations, and many of their number have adopted the profession of Christianity. The Mobile-Natches or Floridan family comprises six principal and independent branches, each of which is subdivided into several tribes: 1st. The Natches, of whom only a scanty remnant now exists, scattered amongst the Creeks, Chickesaws, &c. They were formerly a very powerful race, were worshippers of the sun, and were remarkable for their attainments in the arts of civilized life. 2d. The Muskohges or Creeks, who are the most numerous of the aboriginal tribes who have formed settlements in the United States, and who consist of two principal branches. 1st. The Upper Creeks, or Creeks properly so called, who are the most numerous, and who form a powerful confederation. They inhabit the more elevated districts of Alabama. They have made great progress in the arts of social life, and have schools for the education of their children. 2d. The Lower Creeks, called also Seminoles, who inhabit the plains watered by the Flint in Alabama. They are less advanced in civilization than the other branch of the Creek family. 3d. The Chickesaws who, in conjunction with the Yazoux, inhabit the northern districts of the state of the Mississippi. They were at the beginning of the last century the dominant tribe in that region of America. They are still numerous, and are making rapid progress in the cultivation of their social condition. They possess several large villages, and employ themselves chiefly in agriculture. 4th. The Chactaws or Choctaws, called also Flat-heads, a numerous agricultural tribe who inhabit large villages in the states of Alabama, Mississippi, Louisiana, and Arkansas. They are remarkable for having a written code of laws. 5th. The Cherokees, a numerous tribe, the greater number of whom continue to occupy—in preference to the tract to the W of the Mississippi assigned to them by the United States—their original territory which comprises the NW angle of Georgia, the NE of the state of Alabama and the SE of that of Tennessee. With the exception of the detachment who settled on the Arkansas in 1818, the Cherokees may be characterized as the most enlightened of the independent aboriginal tribes of the New world. Their government is formed on the model of that of the United States. The Mohawk, Huron, or Iroquois family consists of a great variety of tribes, once numerous and powerful, but now reduced to a small residue. The principal of these tribes are: 1st. The Mohawks, who frequent the banks of the Niagara and the bay of the powerful confederation called the Five Nations; it included also the Oneidas, Cayugas, Tuscuroras, Canoys, Mohegans and Nauticokes, and formed a powerful alliance with the English during the Canadian war. The three last-named tribes belong to the Delaware-Chippeways, and are commonly called Stockbridge Indians. The five first were called Maquas by the early Dutch voyagers, and Iroquois by the French. The principal occupations of these tribes consist in agriculture, the rearing of cattle, and a few species of handicraft. They have some schools, and the Mohawks of Canada have adopted the European costume. 2d. The Senecas, who, although only about 1,600 in number, form the largest of the confederated tribes. They live in the states of Ohio and New York. They are skilful artisans, and in dress and manner make a nearer approach to the Whites-especially in the latter state—than any of the other aborigines. 3d. The Hurons, formerly a numerous and powerful tribe to the E of the lake which bears their name, now reduced to about 1,500, who occupy the village of Loretto, 9 m. from Quebec. They profess Catholicism, and devote themselves to agriculture. The Lennappe or Lenape family. Of the numerous tribes which composed this family, and who formerly over-ran the vast region to the E of the Alleghany mountains, from Cape Breton to Cape Hatteras, the principal are: 1st. The Shawanos, formerly a numerous and widely scattered race, of whom the residue now inhabits the banks of the Upper Wabash in Indiana, of the English river, and of the Great Miami in Ohio and Illinois. Of the tribes which this family comprises the most remarkable are: 1st. The Mequachaques, to whom exclusively belongs the charge of the ceremonials of their religious worship. 2d. The Kikkapoos, who are noted for their belligerent propensities. 3d. The Sakis, Sawkees and Ottogamies, called also Foxes, who are the two principal tribes which remain of the numerous branch of the Shawanos who formerly possessed the vast region to the E of the Mississippi, between the Wisconsin and Illinois. They now inhabit the banks of the Upper Mississippi, and of its tributary the Ayooa, and cultivate maize. They are allies of the Sioux, and were long engaged in a war of extermination against the tribes of the Missouri and Illinois. 4th. The Miamis, and (5th.) the Illinois who are the best known tribes belonging to this branch. 6th. The Pottawatameh, who are the most numerous, inhabit the states of Indiana and Illinois. The latter tribe, and (7th.) the Winnebagoes have disposed of, to the United States, nearly the whole of their territory to the S of the Winconsin. 8th. The Lenni-Lennape or Lenopea, called by the English Delawares, and by the French Loups, who were formerly a numerous tribe scattered over the entire area of the United States. Their residue is now to be found only in the states of Indiana and Ohio. 9th. The Mahicanni or Mohicans, and (10th.) the Abenaqui, are the two principal existing branches of a once numerous family who inhabited various districts of New England and New York. They are now to be found, under the designation of Stockbridge Indians, in the eastern extremity of Long Island, and belong to the Mohawk confederation. 11th. The Micmaks or Gaspisiens, formerly very numerous and scattered over the entire extent of the eastern coast of Canada, of New Brunswick, Nova Scotia, and some of the adjacent islands—who now inhabit the SW coast of Nova Scotia and the interior of Newfoundland. The former section profess Christianity, and have made rapid progress in civilization. They are supposed to be descendants of the ancient inhabitants of the mountainous region of Gaspisia to the right of the St. Lawrence, who were famous for their early civilization and possession of the knowledge of Christianity. 12th. The Algonquins, and (13th.) the Chippeways, are the two principal branches of a well known tribe, who are scattered over a great part of Canada, and of the territory of Michigan, and districts Huron and Mandanes in the United States. They use hieroglyphics, and maintain incessant warfare with the Sioux, over whom their possession of fire-arms generally gives them the advantage. 14th. The Kinstenaux, a numerous tribe, who are scattered over the entire area of Lower Canada, over a portion of Labrador, of New Wales, and westerly to the Lake of mountains and Swan river. The Nenawehk, who inhabit the banks of the Saverne. 16th. The Abbitibes, who inhabit the banks of the lake and river which bear their name. They calculate time by nights. 17th. Progressive geography of America.] The most important human discoveries have often originated in trivial and apparently accidental circumstances. He who discovered America was not in quest of new lands, but only purposed to explore a new way to lands already known. The maps of the age in which this great discovery was made were rudely sketched, and most inaccurate in their delineations: they place China, for example, no less than 15 hours, or 225 degrees, eastwards from the western coasts of Europe. This was an error of 85 degrees; but allowing it to have been accurate, it is evident that a westward route to China would be 135 degrees shorter than an eastward voyage, supposing both to be made in a straight line. This early suggested the idea of sailing westwards to the rich countries known by the name of the Indies, and thus opening a shorter passage for the trade which at that time enriched the commercial nations of Europe. Norwegian discoveries.] The earliest claim, however, to the honour of discovering the New world-as it was at first calledis that which has been advanced by Bayer, on the authority of a passage in the Chronicle of Olaus, on behalf of the Norwegians. Iceland was discovered by Norwegian mariners in a. D. 860. In 982, Snorro Sturlæus represents the Norwegians to have advanced as far as the coast of Greenland, and to have "proceeded towards the west," where finding a more attractive coast, on which were some grape-vines, and, in the interior, several hospitable vallies shaded with wood, they gave it the name of Winland or Findland, 1 and settled some colonists there. The commanders of this expedition, Biorn and Lief, lived two centuries before Snorro, according to his own account; and, except from the tradition of the length of the days and nights at the place where they landed, it would be impossible to form any conjecture as to the spot. From this data, however, it would appear to have been about the 48th or 49th degree of northern latitude. Madoc's voyage.] The Welsh bards and historians put in another claim to the honour of discovering America, on behalf of Madoc, one of their princes, who, they affirm, made a voyage to the shores of the New world in the 12th century. There was long a tradition in Wales of the existence of an Indian tribe near the Missouri, who spoke a dialect of the Welsh language. The curious reader may consult Lord Lyttleton's observations on this subject, in his history of England; and an article, by Mr. Pennant, in the 58th vol. of the Philosophical Transactions. Martin Behaim.] A third claim to the honour of discovering America, of earlier date than the first voyage of Columbus, is of equally doubtful authority. Schedel, a German chronologist of the 15th cent., maintains that his countryman, Martin Behaim, having been entrusted, in 1483, with the command of a Portuguese expedition of discovery, made those discoveries the communication of which to his friend, Columbus, first excited that navigator to enter upon his splendid career of discovery. Columbus.] Several seamen, besides Behaim, who had been carried westerly far from their course, are said to have reported that they had seen an island in those remote seas; and it is asserted by Spanish historians that the charts and journals of an old Andalusian pilot who died in his house, unequivocally informed Columbus of the discovery of land far to the westward. Such hints, if they really were given, would doubtless supply his mind with additional arguments in support of his notions; but the strong hope which carried him through an undertaking, in the performance of which he found so many obstacles, seems to have ignorance, required, in addition to an accurate knowledge of the true figure of the globe, a mind free from ordinary prejudices; and the execution of it demanded uncommon courage and perseverance. These qualities of mind were, in a very extraordinary degree, possessed by Christopher Columbus.
