The northern extremity is formed by Ras Morah. These islets are composed of marine alluvies; and some of them rise to the height of 30 ft. above high water mark. The banks of those nearest the coast are generally steep, tideworn, and difficult of access. Kouta contains ruins of houses and fortifications, but is now uninhabited; some are frequented by fishermen; and goats and camels find pasturage upon them. The shores of the bay form an extensive plain, covered with a thick jungle, chiefly of rack-tree, and bounded at the distance of 15 m. by an amphitheatral range of mountains. On a small arm of the bay is the village of Duroro; and a little to the N is that of Madir. The inhabitants of these villages and the neighbouring coast and territory consist of various tribes, each maintaining a state of independence. They are of nomadic habits, and are nominally Mahommedans. A chart of Amphila bay is given by Salt.

AMPHITRITE ISLES, a cluster of five low islets in the Chinese sea, N of the Paracels. They are AMR connected by a reef, and form a chain of about 12 m. in length, of which the central lat. is 16° 56½ N, E long. 112° 19½'.
AMPILLY-LE-SEC, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Côte-d'Or, cant. and 4 m. SW of Chatillon-sur-Seine. Pop. 535.
AMPLEFORTH, a parish in the N. R. of Yorkshire. Area 2,270 acres. Pop. 446.
AMPLEPUIS, a town of France, in the dep. of Rhone, cant. of Thisy. Pop. 4,873. There are some linen and cotton manufactories here.
AMPLIER, a commune of France, in the dep. of Pas-de-Calais, arrond. of Arras. Pop. 555.
AMPLITZ, a town of Prussia, in Brandenburg, 5 m. S of Goben.
AMPNEY-CRUCIS, a parish in Gloucestershire, 3½ m. E by S of Circucester. Area 2,660 acres. Pop. 591.
AMPNEY-DOWN, a parish adjoining the above. Area 2,470 acres. Pop. 425.
AMPOCAN, a town of La Plata, 85 m. NE of San Juan de la Frontera.
AMPORT, a parish in Hampshire, 5 m. W by S of Andover. Area 3,460 acres. Pop. 771.

AMPOSTA, a town of Spain, in Catalonia, on the W bank of the Ebro, 9 m. from its mouth. Ford calls it "a miserable, aguish, mosquito-plagued port." There is a canal from this place to Rapita and the port of Alfaques.
AMPSIN, a commune and village of Belgium, in the prov. of Liege, 3 m. NE of Huy. Pop. 832. Manganese and alum are mined here.

AMPTHILL, a parish and town of Bedfordshire. Area of p. 1,928 acres. Pop. 2,001. The t. is 8 m. S by W of Bedford. Its chief trade is that of the principal market in a rich agricultural district. Its only manufactures are lace and straw-plait.
AMPTON, a parish in Suffolk, 5 m. N by E of Bury St. Edmonds. Area 870 acres. Pop. 147.
AMPUDIA, a village of Spain, in the prov. of Palencia. It was once the see of a bishop. Pop. 400.
AMPUIS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Rhone, cant. of Sainte Colombe. Pop. 1,924.

AMPURIAS, or EMPURIAS, a small seaport of Spain, in Catalonia, prov. of Gerona, on the Fluvia or Llobregat river, near its entrance into Rosas bay; 15 m. NE of Gerona; in N lat. 42° 5', E long. 3° 6'. Pop. 2,200. The Castellon d'Ampurias is at a little distance to the N.
AMPUS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Var, arrond. of Draguignan. Pop. 1,268.
AMRA, a river of Sweden, in the prov. of Jamtland, flowing into the Ragunda, 17 m. E of Stugun.
AMRAH, a village of Arabia, in the Hedjaz, on the route from Bassorah to Mecca, 85 m. NE of Mecca.
AMRAJEE KOTE, a town in Sinde, on the l. bank of the Indus, 16 m. above Tatta.

AMRAN, a small town of Arabia, in a territory of the same name, in the prov. of Tehama, 20 m. NW of Sanaa. It is walled and protected by a castle. -Also a town and fort of Hindostan, prov. of Gujerat, 22 m. SW of Mallia.

AMRAN MOUNTAINS, a range in Afghanistan, forming the eastern buttress of the table-land of Pisheen and Shawl. The Kojuk pass leads through this range, attaining an elevation at its summit of 7,457 ft., which is at least 1,000 ft. below the highest summits of the range.
AMRAVUTTY, a town in the Northern Circars of Hindostan, 20 m. NNW from Guntur.

AMRAWATTY, a large town in the prov. of Berar, Hindostan, 34 m. SE from Ellichpur. It is pleasantly situated in a plain extending along the S bank of the Kistna. Its streets are laid out in straight lines. The most remarkable buildings are the pagodas and mosques, and the rajah's palaces. About 500 yards from its W end are the ruins of Durnacotta, which is said to have been the ancient capital of this district.—Asiatic Journal, 1823.

AMRI, a town of Nubia, on the l. bank of the Nile, about 20 m. above the cataract of Doulga.-Also a village of Sinde, in N lat. 26° 7'.—And a river in Beluchistan, flowing S into the Vehrab. AMRITSIR, [i. e. 'the Pool of immortality,'] formerly called Chak, a town of Hindostan, in the Punjab, 44 m. E from Lahore, the principal place of the religious worship of the Sikhs. It is, on account of its situation between Cabul and Delhi, Cashmere and the Deccan, a place of great trade; and is even supposed to exceed Lahore in population. It is encircled by fortifications of great thickness; but its chief importance is derived from a tulao or sacred pool constructed by Ram Dass, one of the earlier pontiffs of the Sikh faith, who died in 1581. This holy basin, or tank, is 135 paces square, and has in its centre a temple dedicated to the Hindu saint Govind Singh. Under a silken canopy, in this temple, is deposited the saint's book of religion and laws. The voluntary contributions of pilgrims and devotees support this place, to which 600 ukalees or priests are attached. It is in N lat. 31° 33', E long. 74° 48'; 1,312 m. NW from Calcutta. Runjeet Singh constructed a canal from the Ravee to this place. It is 34 m. in length, but is an inefficient work. Manufactures of silks, coarse cloths, and fine goats' wool shawls, exist here.
AMROAH, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Delhi, 22 m. N of Sumbul. There are large plantations of sugar and cotton in the environs; but the district generally is sterile and thinly peopled.
AMROM, or AMROMOE, an isle of Denmark, in the North sea, belonging to Sleswick; in N lat. 54° 38', E long. 8° 29'. Pop. 500.
AMRUS, a town of Lower Egypt, on the right bank of the Rosetta branch of the Nile, 18 m. NW of Menuf; nearly opposite to the mouth of the Buhireh canal.
AMSCHELBERG, a town of Bohemia, in the circle of Beraun, 24 m. S of Prague.
AMSHIR, a town of Asiatic Turkey, in the pashalik of Tripoli, in N lat. 34° 12'.
AMSOING, a village of Luxemburg, on the French frontier, 4 m. E of Virton. The combined Austrians and Prussians here received a severe check from the French, on the 12th of August, 1792.
AMSOLDINGEN, a village of Berne, at the foot of the Stockhorn, 4 m. SW of Thun. Pop. 355.
AMSTADTEN, or Amstetten, a town of Lower Austria, on the road between Vienna and Lintz. It has some iron works. Pop. 900.
AMSTAG, or Amsteg, a village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Uri, 7 m. S of Altdorf. Pop. 260. A large cattle-fair is annually held here.

AMSTEL, a river in North Holland, formed by the confluence of the Drecht and Mydrecht, running through the city of Amsterdam, and discharging its waters into the Het Y, a branch of the Zuyder Zee. It gives to Amsterdam the Latinized name of Amstellodanum.
AMSTELVEEN, a town of Holland, in the prov. of N Holland, 6 m. S of Amsterdam, between the Amstel and the sea of Haarlem. Pop. 5,000. It is well-built, and has some manufactures.

AMSTERDAM, the principal city in the kingdom of Holland, in the prov. of N Holland, at the confluence of the Amstel with an arm of the Zuyder Zee called the Het Y, or Wye; in N lat. 52° 23′ 17", E long. 4° 54'; 32 m. NE of the Hague; 107 m. N of Brussels; 340 m. N by E of Paris; and 5 W of the Zuyder Zee. By railway it is distant from Haarlem AMS from Rotterdam 57 m., from Utrecht 23 m., and from Arnheim 56 m. Besides its public buildings, Amsterdam, according to Busching, contained, in 1732, 26,385 dwelling-houses, and several extensive ship-yards, and manufactories of ropes, cordage, tobacco, &c., with a pop. of 200,000. The number of inhabitants in 1796 was 217,000; in 1808, 208,000, among whom were 20,000 Jews. In 1820, however, there were but 180,000; of whom 90,000 were Calvinists, 38,000 Catholics, and 30,000 Lutherans. On the 1st of January, 1830, the pop. was estimated at 202,364; in 1843 at 212,000, of whom 24,000 were Jews. The inhabitants are in general healthy, industrious, moral, and religious. Their institutions for the relief of poverty and prevention of distress are on a liberal scale, and under excellent regulation. Education is general; and there are several literary institutions as well as hospitals, almshouses, and a variety of charitable establishments. Though distinguished for attention to religious duties, and professing in general that form of Christianity known by the name of Calvinism, they are remarkably free from intolerance or even theological controversy, -a feature of character which may perhaps have arisen from the circumstances of the earlier history of the city. Though in the early part of the revolutionary war with Spain, it was found on the side of the sanguinary duke of Alva, this was probably owing to the influence of the men to whom the government had been committed. In 1578, it joined the Confederation, and afforded a ready asylum to the persecuted of all nations. The influx of foreigners, skilled in the various arts and manufactures, led to subsequent wealth and prosperity, - while the actual observation and experience of the evils, political and moral, consequent on intolerance, would naturally incline them to the cultivation of a temper more in accordance with genuine Christianity, and in no degree inconsistent with that depth of conviction and strength of character which had subjected the refugée to exile, and which had through his means been diffused among those whose hospitality had afforded him a shelter. On account of the lowness of the site—originally a salt marsh—the greater part of this city is built on piles driven to a depth of 40 or 50 ft. Its general form is that of a crescent, or rather of a theatre in which the port occupies the place of the stage; the streets are in straight lines, and some of them are lined with rows of trees. A large semicircular moat runs round the walls; and five canals, which supply all the rest, describe, in the interior of the city, five parallel curves; while by the intersection of its canals, A. is divided into 95 little islands, which are connected and braced together as it were into one city by no less than 660 bridges. It is from 8 to 9 m. in circumference, and covers a space of 900 acres. "If it be not heresy," says a lively anonymous writer, "to compare Venice with any other city in the world, I should be inclined to say that Amsterdam is the Venice of the north. Like the queen of the Laguna, the queen of the Zuyderzee is seated on a throne of islands girded by innumerable canals. But the resemblance goes no further. In the Dutch Venice we must not look for the palaces and gondolas which confer the twofold character of grandeur and romance on the ancient city of the doges. The palaces of Amsterdam are small red brick houses, with white angles and pointed tops; her gondolas are large clumsy-looking boats, for they are usually laden with butter and cheese. But the quays of Amsterdam are delightfully shaded by rows of verdant trees, an ornament which Venice cannot boast. In the superior street, the brick of which the houses are built is left of its natural red colour; but in the better quarters 12 m., from Leyden 30 m., from the Hague 40 m., of the town, such as the Keyser Gracht, the Heeren Gracht, and the Cingel, the outsides of the houses are painted and varnished as carefully as the pannels of the interior. Unfortunately, good taste does not always dictate the choice of colours: some houses are blue, others green or yellow, whilst the corners and peaked tops being plastered white, and ornamented with rude sculpture, increase the singularity of their appearance. The houses have invariably three windows on each storey in front; some have more; those which have five are houses of the very highest class. All are built on piles; for the stratum of earth is very thin, and water is found 7 or 8 ft. below the surface. For a house of ordinary magnitude, about 100 piles are necessary; for public buildings, thousands are requisite. I have heard it alleged, that, in the erection of the palace, no less than 13,700 were employed; and for the navy office 8,000. Thus, building is a costly undertaking in Holland. Building a house is a secondary consideration; it is necessary to begin by building the ground on which the house is to stand. This last-mentioned process is not always very easily accomplished, for the ground often gives way during the progress of the structure raised upon it. For example; it has been found impossible to complete the steeple of the Nieuw Kerk, on the Dam, because the edifice sank in proportion as it was raised. This mode of building is attended by many serious inconveniences. The canals become stagnant, which renders the water fetid in summer, and impure at all seasons: but it rarely happens that means are taken to set the water in motion, or to procure fresh supplies by the aid of sluices, lest the action of the current, by loosening the piles, should endanger the stability of every house in the city." [New Monthly Magazine.] In earlier times A. was a strong fortress. Its twenty-six bastions, and its means of inundating the country, made even Lous cautious of attacking it; but, in 1787, when threatened by a Prussian army of only moderate size, it was obliged to surrender after the capture of the fortified villages in the vicinity. In consequence of the changes which have taken place in the mode of conducting sieges, A. can now be defended only by the inundation of the surrounding country; and the ancient fortifications are now converted into boulevards and planted with trees, while the bastions are occupied by gigantic mills which seem to overshadow the city. On the side towards Haarlem, the city is at present protected by the sluice of Halfwegen; and on the eastern side, by the fortress of Naarden. With the exception of two great Gothic churches, and five or six steeples remarkable only for their deafening chimes and Spanish architecture, the public buildings of Amsterdam are not distinguishable from private houses. The most remarkable is the palace. The square on which this edifice stands, and which it almost entirely fills, is called the Dam, and forms the central point of the city. This palace was, in fact, the town-hall in the time of the republic; and its architecture was originally in the purest Gothic style. It was, however, modernized and disfigured for the purpose of being converted into a residence for Louis Bonaparte. It is still used as a royal palace, and the king resides in it when he visits A. The throne-room is said to be larger than any similar apartment in Europe. The walls are faced with white marble; but its finest ornaments are some Spanish flags formerly wrested from foreign despotism by the About 3,500 vessels enter A. annually from the sea; hands of liberty. This building was begun after the peace of Westphalia, in 1648; and was finished in 1655. In the vaults under the stadt-house are deposited the treasures of the bank of Amsterdam. This splendid building stands upon 13,659 piles; and is 282 ft. long, 235 ft. wide, and 116 ft. high, without reckoning the high tower. The interior has been decorated by the Dutch painters and sculptors of the 17th cent., with their master-pieces. The exchange, which was built between 1608 and 1613, and rested upon five vaulted arches, under which the Amstel flows into the Damrack, was 250 ft. long, and 140 wide. Its foundations having given way, the building has been demolished, and a new exchange erected in front of the palace. The churches are numerous. The Dutch Reformed have ten; the French, one; the English, one; the Lutherans, three; the Roman Catholics, eighteen; even the Greeks and Armenians have a church; and the Jews seven synagogues. The principal bridge over the Amstel, where it enters the city, is 610 ft. in length, by 64 in width, with 11 arches, through which large ships easily pass. Among the literary institutions is the athenæum or college, an academy of the fine arts, and a school of navigation. With much that is beautiful and pleasing, and with a trade which affords the means of support to every industrious man, A. has the disadvantage of a damp atmosphere, and, as already noticed, an offensive mephitic smell which often rises in summer from the canals. The Amstel at its entrance into the city is 11 inches below the mean level of the German ocean, and the lowest tide is only 1½ ft. lower than the Amstel. It is therefore evident that the canals can be emptied only at low water, and that but partially. It suffers, also, from the want of good spring-water, and from the inconvenience of very high and narrow dwelling-houses, occasioned by its crowded population. The Helder or new canal, extending from A. to Nieuwediep in the Texal, the extreme point of North Holland, 26 ft. in depth, is of great advantage to A. It removes some of the chief impediments to the commerce of the city, viz., the necessity which existed of unloading large vessels before they could enter the harbour, and of encountering the passage through the Zuyderzee, which was peculiarly difficult with contrary winds. This canal, constructed at an expense of £1,500,000 between 1819 and 1825, extends from Amsterdam to Nieuwediep. The distance between the extreme points is 41 m.; but the canal is about 51 m. long. The breadth at the surface is 123 ft. 7 in.; at the bottom 30 ft. 10 in.; the depth 20 ft. 6 in. It passes through a somewhat marshy country; and touches Purmerend and Alkmaar. Like the Dutch canals generally, its level is that of the high tides of the sea, from which it receives its supply of water. The only locks which it requires, of course, are two tide-locks at its extremities; but there are also two sluices with flood-gates in the intermediate space. The locks and sluices are double, that is to say, there are two in the breadth of the canal. The canal is wide enough to admit of one frigate passing another. The time spent in tracking vessels from the Helder to A. varies according to their size and the means of haulage; fly-boats, with six relays of 4 horses each, making it in 10 hours; whilst large East Indiamen require two, three, or four days, according to the wind. The harbour is spacious and secure, and the largest ships can approach the quays and warehouses. It has recently been much improved by the formation of additional docks and basins. Two enormous dykes serve the double purpose of protecting the city from inundation, and gaining from the Y-pronounced Ey -two capacious basins or dykes capable of holding nearly 1,000 vessels and closed by large sluice-gates. and about 350 by the Rhine, the Meuse, and the various canals. In 1840, 4,177 vessels, of a total tonnage of 437,695 tons, entered A.; and 3,981 cleared out. Of the former number 1,062 were English. A. has long been known as a city of great commercial importance. Throughout the 17th and the early part of the 18th cent., it might be regarded as the metropolis of the commercial world; various circumstances led to its decline from so distinguished pre-eminence, particularly the rapid strides made by Great Britain, and ultimately the subjugation of Holland by France. After the peace it regained some of its former importance, and it at present holds a prominent place in the mercantile world. Its manufactures comprise woollen, linen, cotton, silk, sail-cloth, sugar refineries, borax, sal ammoniac, sulphur, &c. It contains distilleries, breweries, tanneries, borax refineries, tobacco manufactories, smalt manufactories, iron founderies, &c. Ship-building is carried on extensively; and the art of cutting diamonds and other precious stones has attained great perfection. The principal imports are sugar, coffee, spices, tobacco, cotton, tea, indigo, cochineal, wine, brandy, wool, grain, timber, pitch and tar, hemp and flax, iron, hides, linen, cotton, and woollen stuffs, hardware, rock salt, tin plates, coal, dried fish. The exports consist of cheese and butter,—which articles at one period brought 1,000,000 of ducats annually to A., -madder, clover, rape, hemp, and flax seeds, rape and linseed oils, Dutch linen, &c., the produce of Holland; of spices, coffee, sugar, cotton, tea, tobacco, from the Dutch colonies and East Indies; and of grain, linens, timber, wool, wines, brandy, &c., from different parts of Europe. The value of the imports and exports amounts to from £3,500,000 to £4,000,000. In 1832, the imports were £4,794,000; the exports £3,081,000. In 1840 the imports amounted to 95,339,500 florins, or £7,944,958, and the exports to 74,701,000 florins, or £6,225,083. The imports from England amounted to £722,600; the exports to England to £735,229. The Eastern Dutch Indies imported to the value of £2,939,667; and received exports to the value of £761,623. In the same year (1840) it exported via the Rhine 1,027,611 quintals of goods, of which the principal items were sugar and coffee. While the total imports and exports of A. in 1840 amounted to 170,050,500 florins; those of Rotterdam amounted to 172,542,500. The old bank of Amsterdam was founded in 1609, and ceased to exist in 1796, at the time of the French invasion. The present bank of the Netherlands was established in 1814. The merchants of A. were formerly extensive dealers in bills of exchange, and all sorts of funded property and government securities, and business of this nature is still carried on extensively in this city. The whale fisheries are also of importance. The government of A. is vested in a council called the vroedschap, of 36 members, in whom the supreme power is lodged. The office is held during life, and vacancies are filled by the survivors. This body elects the chief magistrates, named echevins or burgomasters, -a rank somewhat similar to that of aldermen. The number of these is 12; they have the direction of all public works, and hold the keys of the city-bank. The military protection of the town is intrusted to the militia, consisting of 60 companies of from 200 to 300 men each. Jews and Anabaptists are excluded from this body, as they are not allowed to bear arms; they are, however, obliged to contribute to the support of the city-guard, consisting of 1,400 soldiers, and to the night-watch, which patroles the streets and calls the hours. In addition to this night-patrole, trumpeters are stationed in every church-steeple, who sound every half-hour, and, in case of fire, ring the alarm-bells, and direct inquirers to the place. "Holland," says a writer already quoted, "owes her existence to the spirit of association: it cannot, therefore, be matter of surprise that that spirit should have taken deep root in the people. A. is distinguished above every city in the world for the number of its liberal and philanthropic institutions, all of which have been founded and are supported by voluntary gifts. To enumerate them would occupy too much space; but it is sufficient to observe that the arts, literature, science, agriculture, and commerce, have each their academies. Every kind of human misery and infirmity is succoured and relieved, not by the official and paid charity which waits till it hears the appeal of misfortune, but by that spontaneous benevolence which seeks for and finds objects deserving relief. But the city of A., prosperous as it is in appearance, is, like every other great capital, afflicted with the scourge of pauperism. It is calculated that 20,000 persons subsist by daily alms; but not one of these is seen begging in the streets. Houses of refuge are open for those who can work, and hospitals for the sick and disabled. In Holland, the spirit of association pervades, as it does in England, every class of the community. Among working-people societies are formed, the members of which pay a moderate weekly subscription, and thereby insure to themselves succour in case of sickness, and some little provision for their widows in the event of their death." This celebrated commercial city, at one time the most important in Europe, was, at the beginning of the 13th cent., only a fishing-village belonging to the lords of Amstel. About the middle of that cent. it became a small town, and obtained a municipal government. It was at this period called Amstelredamme In 1296, it was suddenly attacked and plundered by the neighbouring Kennemers, on account of the participation of Gisbert of Amstel in the murder of Count Floris of Holland. In this way A., together with the surrounding district, came under the rule of the counts of Holland, who subsequently granted the city many privileges. It soon acquired an important commerce in the Baltic sea; and, in the 16th cent., was a place of considerable wealth. The transition from the bondage of its feudal lords, to the state of subjects of the counts of Holland, was the origin of its prosperity. In 1482 it was walled. In 1560, Guicciardini says of its port, "Ships are constantly seen in great numbers coming in and going out, not only to and from other parts of the Netherlands, but of France, England, Germany, Spain, Portugal, Poland, Livonia, Sweden," &c. In 1585, after Antwerp had fallen a second time under the dominion of Spain, its extensive commerce was transferred to A., and the western or new part of the city was built. In 1622 it contained 100,000 inhabitants. Its increasing importance now awakened the envy of its neighbours. In 1587, Leicester attempted to take it by treachery; and William II., in 1650, by surprise. Both attempts were frustrated by the burgomasters, who now acquired so much weight in the assembly of the states-general, that their authority, during the greater part of the 17th cent., rivalled that of the hereditary stadt-holder. During this period of prosperity, A. accumulated great wealth, especially after the institution of its bank in 1609; it was the emporium for all the productions of the East and West; and its harbour was always full of ships. Its trade, however, was obstructed by the sand-bank between the Texel and the extreme point of N Holland, on account of which large vessels could not enter without unloading part of their cargoes into lighters. Vessels, moreover, could not sail from the Zuyder Zee, near the Texel, except with certain winds. In 1653, the war with England did such injury to its commerce that 4,000 houses in the city were left unoccupied; and, it is said, the exchange became overgrown with grass. In 1656, A. was greatly enlarged, and surrounded by new walls with gates. Commerce revived and flourished even during the unquiet period from 1790 to 1794, with the exception of the time of the English war from 1781 to 1782. The forced alliance of Holland with France, which obliged her to follow the French policy, operated to the great disadvantage of Amsterdam. Louis Bonaparte endeavoured to restore the trade of Holland by means of grants and privileges, and even transferred his residence and the seat of government to Amsterdam in 1808; but the first measure only irritated Napoleon against Holland; and the other, though it opened some new sources of trade, was followed by various disadvantages. The complete incorporation of Holland with France in 1810, entirely annihilated the foreign trade of Amsterdam; and many other measures—as, for instance, the introduction of the monopoly of tobacco and of the droits réunis, as they were called—were very injurious to the domestic trade of the city. The revolution of 1813 restored the business of A.; and since that time its commerce has increased very considerably.— A. was the birth-place of the poet-soldier Bræckhuysen in 1649; of Spinosa in 1632; of the naturalist Swammerdam in 1637; and of the jurisconsult Bilderdyk in 1750.-Wagenaar, Beschryving von Amsterdam. Amsterd., 1760-67, 13 vols. 8vo.-Dictionnaire Geographique.—New Monthly Magazine.—Murray's Hand-book.— Macgregor's Commercial Tariffs, Part 6.

AMSTERDAM, a town in Montgomery co. state of New York, U. S., 32 m. W of Albany, on the N side of the Mohawk river, on the Utica and Shenectady railroad. Pop. 1,700.—Also a township in Virginia, 181 m. W by S of Richmond.—Also a township in Hinds co. Mississippi.—Also a fort on the SW coast of Curaçoa island, in N lat. 12° 10'.

AMSTERDAM, an island of the Indian ocean, in S lat. 38° 53', E long. 77° 37' [Stokes]; about midway between Australia and Madagascar; first visited by Van Vlaming, a Dutch navigator, in 1697; and explored, in 1793, by the gentlemen attached to Macartney's embassy to China. Its length is upwards of 4 m.; its breadth about 21; and it attains an elevation of 2,760 ft. A fertile, but very soft and spongy soil covers the island, which bears unquestionable marks of a volcanic origin; and several springs of hot water exist here, of which the average heat is about 212°. On the shores immense numbers of seals were formerly taken by the Americans. The fishers generally leave a number of men on the island, and return to take them after they have had time to collect a sufficient number of skins. The neighbouring sea abounds in fish, especially perch and cray.- Also a small, uninhabited island in the North sea, near the NW coast of Spitzbergen. Dutch vessels resort thither towards the end of their whale-fishery. N lat. 79° 46', E long. 9° 40'.—An island off the N coast of Papua, in S lat. 0° 17%. - A small island on the NW coast of Ceylon, 5 m. long, and 2 in breadth, in N lat. 9° 50', E long. 8° 1'.

AMSTERDAM (New), a seaport of British Guayana, near the mouth of the Berbice, at its confluence with the Canjee, along the bank of which it extends about 13 m.; in N lat. 6° 20', W long. 57° 15'. It is the seat of the Berbice government; and bears the epithet New to distinguish it from OLD AMSTERDAM, an early settlement, 60 or 70 m. up the river.
AMSTETTEN, a town of Austria, on the l. bank of the Ips. Pop. 617.
AMSTOSS, a village of Switzerland, cant. of Appenzell, on the border of the Rheinthal, 4 m. SW of Rheinek. Frederick, Duke of Austria, was defeated here by the Swiss in 1405.

AMTCHITKA, or AMCHATKA, one of the Aleutian islands, about 60 m. in length, mountainous, and bearing little vegetation. A cluster of islets lies on its N side; and off its eastern and western extremities are numerous rocks.
AMT GEHREN, a town of Schwarzburg-Sondershausen, on the Wohlrose, at the foot of the Thuringerwald. Pop. 1,542.
AMTSZELL, or AMTZELL, a town of Wurtemburg, in the circle of the Bodensee, 8 m. NW of Wangen. Pop. 2,200.
AMU. See Oxus.
AMUCHTA, one of the Aleutian islands, about 27 m. in length. There is a volcano upon it.
AMUCU. See AMICU.
AMUL. See AMOL.
AMULRIE, a village of Perthshire, on the Bran river, 11 m. N of Crieff. It is intersected by the great military road between Inverness and Stirling.
